lessons.title: Introduction to Neural Networks
lessons.content:
# Introduction to Neural Networks

A neural network is a machine for building **useful intermediate representations**. You feed in raw numbers (pixels, sensor readings, word IDs), and after a few transformations the network produces outputs you care about (a class label, a price, the next token). The magic isn’t any single step—it’s the *stacking* of simple steps into a **computational graph** that can be tuned from data.

## The neuron: a tiny calculator with knobs
A **neuron** takes an input vector \(x\in\mathbb{R}^D\), combines it with learnable **weights** \(w\) and a **bias** \(b\), and produces a number:

\[
z = w\cdot x + b
\]

On its own, this is just a linear function. If we only used linear functions, stacking layers would still be linear overall—so the network wouldn’t gain expressive power.

That’s why we add an **activation function** \(\phi\), giving:

\[
a = \phi(z)
\]

Common activations include ReLU (\(\max(0,z)\)) and tanh. Their job is simple: introduce nonlinearity so the network can bend and fold space.

## Layers: many neurons in parallel
A **layer** is a bundle of neurons that all look at the same input and produce a vector of outputs. In matrix form:

\[
\text{layer}(x) = \phi(Wx + b)
\]

- \(W\) is a matrix of weights (one row per neuron)
- \(b\) is a bias vector
- \(\phi\) is applied elementwise

## Deep networks: repeated “linear + nonlinearity”
A feedforward neural network (often an MLP) is just layers composed:

\[
x \rightarrow \phi(W_1x+b_1) \rightarrow \phi(W_2\,\cdot + b_2) \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{scores}
\]

A helpful mental model: each layer **re-represents** the data. Early layers might separate “rough shape” from “texture”; later layers can make the classes almost linearly separable. Depth gives you multiple chances to “untangle” the data.

## The forward pass: running the graph
The **forward pass** is simply evaluating these equations to get an output.

Here’s a tiny NumPy-style forward pass for a 2-layer network:

```python
import numpy as np

def relu(x):
    return np.maximum(0, x)

# x: (D,)
# W1: (H, D), b1: (H,)
# W2: (C, H), b2: (C,)

def forward(x, W1, b1, W2, b2):
    h = relu(W1 @ x + b1)   # hidden activations
    scores = W2 @ h + b2    # output (often called logits)
    return scores
```

Those final **scores** (often “logits”) are typically turned into probabilities (e.g., with softmax) or used directly for regression.

## Why activations matter (intuition)
Without activations, every layer is just a matrix multiply and bias—so the whole network collapses into one big matrix multiply. Activations prevent that collapse. Geometrically, they let the network repeatedly warp the input space so that a simple final decision boundary (like a line or hyperplane) becomes enough.

## Summary
Neural networks are computational graphs built from layers of neurons, where each layer performs a linear transformation followed by a nonlinearity. The forward pass is just evaluating this chain, producing outputs from inputs through progressively more useful representations.

## Recommended Reading
- [Neural Networks, Manifolds, and Topology](https://colah.github.io/posts/2014-03-NN-Manifolds-Topology/) — geometric intuition for what layers are doing to data.
- [CS231n: Neural Networks Part 2](https://cs231n.github.io/neural-networks-2/#reg) — clear view of “linear + nonlinearity” and practical setup details.
- [micrograd](https://github.com/karpathy/micrograd) — tiny codebase that makes the computational graph idea concrete.
- [nn-zero-to-hero](https://github.com/karpathy/nn-zero-to-hero) — hands-on progression from neurons to full MLPs.
lessons.summary: Neural networks map inputs to outputs by composing layers of neurons, each doing a linear transform followed by a nonlinear activation. The forward pass evaluates this chain, creating progressively more useful representations for the final prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance dependency for 'making' (layer 5).

Different colors represent different attention heads.

image_metadata.caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 involved in anaphora resolution (layer 5).

image_metadata.caption: Sharp attention from 'its' resolving anaphora in heads 5 and 6 (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Image 5 to highlight the contrast between broad and sharp attention; use when explaining how attention heads can specialize and what 'sharp' attention distributions mean for model interpretability.
image_metadata.description: Bottom panel of anaphora resolution visualization: isolated attention lines from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to the antecedent noun, demonstrating coreference resolution behavior.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence-structural patterns (layer 5, head A).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing what attention heads learn and model interpretability; pair with Image 8 to illustrate that different heads learn complementary structural patterns.
image_metadata.description: First of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing attention patterns that appear to reflect syntactic sentence structure, with one head attending in a pattern consistent with a specific grammatical relationship.

image_metadata.caption: A second encoder self-attention head learning different structural patterns (layer 5, head B).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 7 as a direct comparison; use to reinforce why multiple heads are beneficial and to discuss how the model distributes different linguistic tasks across heads.
image_metadata.description: Second of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing a different attention pattern from the first head, demonstrating that distinct heads specialize in different structural or semantic tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Why √dₖ? Scaling prevents softmax saturation as dimension grows. (Outcome School)

image_metadata.description: A mathematical diagram illustrating the derivation and justification of the √dₖ scaling factor used in scaled dot-product attention, likely showing variance analysis of dot products as dimensionality dₖ grows and how dividing by √dₖ stabilizes the variance to 1.

image_metadata.description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data point clouds: original data (left), mean-subtracted/centered data (middle), and PCA-rotated data (right), illustrating the geometric effect of each preprocessing step.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining PCA, when introducing whitening as the next preprocessing step, and when discussing its trade-offs including noise sensitivity.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data clouds: PCA-rotated data (left), whitened data with equal variance in all dimensions (middle), and a noisy whitened version (right), illustrating the effect of whitening and its noise-amplification weakness.

image_metadata.caption: PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students ask how PCA/whitening applies to real image data, or after the abstract 2D visualizations to ground the concepts in a practical computer vision context.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches: showing original image patches, their PCA components (eigenvectors as images), and the resulting whitened patches, demonstrating the transformation on real image data.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing dropout as a regularization technique, especially after covering L1/L2 regularization, to contrast structural regularization approaches with weight-penalty approaches.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagram of a neural network without dropout (left, all connections active) versus with dropout (right, some neurons and their connections randomly removed/crossed out), illustrating the dropout regularization technique.

image_metadata.caption: Raw 2D dataset: two curves the network must classify. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: At the very start of introducing neural network classification; use as the 'problem statement' before showing any network architecture or transformation
image_metadata.description: A 2D scatter plot showing two classes of data points arranged as two curves on a plane, color-coded to distinguish the two classes

image_metadata.caption: Hidden layers enable non-linear decision boundaries on the same dataset. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing the linear classifier result; use to motivate why hidden layers matter by visually demonstrating the gain in expressive power
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization showing how a neural network with hidden layers classifies every point in the input space, displaying a non-linear decision boundary separating the two curve classes

image_metadata.caption: Example neural network architecture with input, hidden, and output layers. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing the network structure being analyzed; reference back to it whenever discussing what happens 'at each layer' during the transformation visualizations
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a small neural network architecture showing input layer, one or more hidden layers, and output layer with labeled nodes and connections

image_metadata.caption: Data in its raw input representation before any network transformation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the idea that each layer creates a new representation; show this as 'Layer 0' before revealing what the hidden layer does to the same data
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization of the dataset as seen in the input (raw) representation — the original space before any layer transformation — showing the two curve classes

image_metadata.caption: Same data after first hidden layer transformation — a new learned representation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after showing the input-space visualization (Image 4); ask students to identify what changed and why that change helps with linear separability
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization of the same dataset after transformation by the first hidden layer, showing how the data distribution has changed shape in the new representation space

image_metadata.caption: Continuous application of a tanh layer gradually deforms the data manifold. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how a single layer transforms data; use to bridge from the discrete layer-by-layer view to the topological interpretation of network layers
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing the continuous transformation of 2D data as a tanh neural network layer is gradually applied, visualizing the smooth deformation of the data manifold

image_metadata.caption: Four hidden layers successfully untangle slightly entangled spirals into linearly separable classes. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the continuous layer transformation concept; use as a success case before contrasting with the failure case on more entangled spirals
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network attempting to classify two slightly entangled spirals, visualizing the progressive transformation through hidden layers until the spirals become linearly separable

image_metadata.caption: A low-dimensional network fails to untangle more entangled spirals — topology wins. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Directly after the successful spiral case (Image 7); the contrast drives home that topology imposes hard limits, motivating the formal lower-bound argument on network width
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network failing to classify two more heavily entangled spirals, with the network unable to achieve linear separability despite multiple layers

image_metadata.caption: Topologically challenging dataset: class A surrounded by class B in 2D. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the formal topology and classification section; use to make the abstract set definitions A={x|d(x,0)<1/3} and B concrete before walking through the proof
image_metadata.description: A 2D plot showing the topology-based classification problem: class A as a filled disk centered at the origin and class B as an annular ring surrounding it, illustrating a topologically non-trivial dataset

image_metadata.caption: Two hidden units cannot topologically separate the surrounded dataset — stuck at ~80%. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After presenting the impossibility proof; use as empirical confirmation that the theoretical lower bound on hidden units is real and observable in practice
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a neural network with only two hidden units attempting and failing to classify the surrounded-disk dataset, getting stuck in an unproductive local minimum

image_metadata.caption: 3D dataset where class A is surrounded by B, preventing hyperplane separation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the proof that standard layers fail for topologically entangled datasets; use to make the abstract argument about determinants and homeomorphisms concrete.
image_metadata.description: A 3D visualization showing two classes of data points (A and B) where one class surrounds the other in 3D space, illustrating a topological classification problem that cannot be solved by a hyperplane in lower dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Third hidden unit lifts 1D entangled classes into linearly separable 2D space. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the 1D classification problem (A = [-1/3, 1/3], B = two outer intervals) to illustrate how a third hidden unit resolves the topological obstruction.
image_metadata.description: A 1D-to-2D transformation visualization showing how a third hidden unit maps the 1D dataset (intervals A and B on a line) into a 2D representation where the two classes become linearly separable.

image_metadata.caption: Animation of neural network learning to separate 1D topological classes during training. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during discussion of how individual hidden units learn threshold functions (firing when x > -1/2 or x > 1/2); use to connect the mathematical description to the visual learning process.
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing the training process of a neural network transforming a 1D dataset into a 2D representation, visualizing how the network learns to map the input intervals A and B into a separable configuration.

image_metadata.caption: Two linked rings: a topological obstruction requiring a 4th dimension to separate. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from the 'one class surrounds another' scenario to more general topological obstructions; use to motivate why neural networks need sufficient dimensionality to untangle manifolds.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of two linked rings (a Hopf link or similar link) in 3D space, representing two interlinked 1D manifolds (classes A and B) that cannot be separated without a 4th dimension.

image_metadata.caption: Linked vs. unlinked tori: neural networks untangle manifolds via ambient isotopy. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how neural network layer transformations correspond to ambient isotopies; use to explain why 3 hidden units suffice to classify an unlink but not a true link.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side or comparative visualization showing a linked pair of tori (topologically entangled) alongside an unlinked configuration, illustrating the concept of an unlink and the role of neural network layers as ambient isotopies.

image_metadata.caption: Tangled manifold illustrating why smooth untangling requires contractive regularization. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the failure modes of neural networks on topologically complex data and the motivation for contractive regularization; connects the 2n+2 dimension theorem to practical training concerns.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of a tangled or knotted manifold, illustrating the geometric complexity that arises when a neural network attempts to separate entangled data manifolds, potentially showing high-derivative regions or near-discontinuities.

image_metadata.caption: Vector field deforming a grid, illustrating a proposed manifold-aware neural network layer. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the proposed vector-field-based layer architecture; use to contrast with standard affine transformations and illustrate how anchor-point interpolation could enable more flexible manifold manipulation.
image_metadata.description: A grid deformation visualization showing a vector field applied to a 2D grid, where arrows indicate the direction and magnitude of spatial deformation, illustrating how a learned vector field can reshape the data manifold.

image_metadata.caption: Bubble deformation from a two-anchor vector field layer formula. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)) to help students connect the mathematical expression to its geometric effect on the data space.
image_metadata.description: A 'bubble' deformation of a grid showing a localized, radially symmetric vector field that pushes or pulls a region of space outward, corresponding to the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)).

lessons.title: Backpropagation & Gradient Descent
lessons.content:
# Backpropagation & Gradient Descent

Training a neural network is basically one repeated question:

> “If I nudge this weight a tiny bit, does the loss go up or down?”

If we can answer that for **every** weight, we can improve the network by stepping downhill. The trick is doing it *efficiently*—without re-running the whole model separately for each parameter.

## The setup: a loss sitting on top of a computation graph

A forward pass is just a chain of computations:

- linear layers (dot products)
- nonlinearities (ReLU/tanh)
- a **loss function** that turns predictions into a single number (badness)

You can picture this as a directed acyclic graph (DAG): values flow forward to produce the loss.

## Gradient descent: “walk downhill” in parameter space

Let \(L\) be the loss and \(w\) a weight. The **gradient** \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}\) tells you the local slope.

**Gradient descent** updates parameters by

\[
 w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}
\]

where \(\eta\) is the learning rate. Too big and you bounce around; too small and training crawls.

## Backpropagation: the chain rule, organized

The core math is the **chain rule**:

If \(y = f(u)\) and \(u = g(x)\), then

\[
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du}\frac{du}{dx}
\]

Backprop is just this idea applied to the whole graph, but in a clever order:

1. Do a forward pass and cache intermediate values.
2. Start from the loss (where the gradient is “known”).
3. Move backward, computing **local derivatives** and multiplying them into the running gradient.

The magic is **reverse-mode autodiff**: one backward sweep gives gradients for *all* parameters, roughly costing about the same as a few forward passes—not “one forward pass per weight.”

## A tiny concrete example (micrograd-style)

Here’s the vibe: build a graph, run forward, then call `backward()` to fill in `.grad` everywhere.

```python
from micrograd.engine import Value

# a tiny "model": L = (w*x + b)^2
w = Value(2.0)
x = Value(-3.0)
b = Value(0.5)

y = w * x + b
L = y ** 2

L.backward()
print(L.data)      # forward value
print(w.grad)      # dL/dw
print(b.grad)      # dL/db
```

What happened conceptually?

- The square contributes a factor of \(2y\).
- The linear part contributes \(\frac{\partial y}{\partial w} = x\) and \(\frac{\partial y}{\partial b} = 1\).
- Backprop multiplies these along the paths: \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = 2y \cdot x\).

## Why this enables “hidden” layers to learn features

Once you can compute gradients for *internal* weights, you can train networks with **hidden units**. Those hidden units aren’t directly supervised, but they get a learning signal through the loss—so they can evolve into useful intermediate features that make the final task easier.

## Summary

Backpropagation is an efficient way to compute \(\nabla L\) for every parameter by applying the chain rule backward through the computation graph. Gradient descent then uses those gradients to iteratively update weights in the direction that reduces the loss.

## Recommended Reading

- [micrograd](https://github.com/karpathy/micrograd) — A tiny autograd engine that makes backprop feel tangible.
- [nn-zero-to-hero](https://github.com/karpathy/nn-zero-to-hero) — Hands-on training and manual backprop exercises.
- [CS231n: Neural Networks Part 2](https://cs231n.github.io/neural-networks-2/#reg) — Practical notes around losses and training deep nets.
- [Learning representations by back-propagating errors](https://www.nature.com/articles/323533a0) — The classic paper that popularized backprop for multilayer nets.
lessons.summary: Backpropagation computes gradients of the loss with respect to every weight efficiently by running the chain rule backward through the computation graph. Gradient descent then uses these gradients to update parameters and minimize the loss.

lessons.title: Optimization Algorithms
lessons.content:
# Optimization Algorithms

Training a neural network is basically one repeated move: **take the gradient** of the loss with respect to parameters, then **nudge parameters downhill**. The entire art is in *how* you nudge.

A good optimizer is like a good hiking strategy in fog: you don’t just step “downhill”—you also smooth out noise, avoid zig-zagging, and take bigger steps when the terrain is gentle.

## The baseline: SGD

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** updates parameters using gradients computed on a minibatch:

\[
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, g_t \quad \text{where } g_t = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)
\]

- **\(\eta\)** is the **learning rate**: too small → slow; too big → unstable.
- Minibatch gradients are noisy, so SGD tends to “bounce” as it descends.

## Momentum: stop fighting the noise

**Momentum** keeps a running “velocity” vector that accumulates consistent gradient directions:

\[
v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + (1-\beta) g_t, \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_t
\]

Intuition: if gradients keep pointing roughly the same way, momentum builds speed; if they oscillate, momentum averages them out. This often helps traverse narrow valleys where plain SGD would jitter side-to-side.

## Adam: momentum + per-parameter step sizes

Deep nets often have parameters with very different gradient scales. **Adam** addresses this by tracking two moving averages:

- **First moment** (mean): momentum-like average of gradients
- **Second moment** (uncentered variance): average of squared gradients

In practice, Adam computes:

- \(m_t\): running average of gradients
- \(v_t\): running average of squared gradients
- then updates each parameter with a step roughly like \(m_t / (\sqrt{v_t}+\epsilon)\)

Key intuition: if a parameter’s gradients are consistently large, Adam *automatically* shrinks its effective learning rate; if gradients are small or sparse, Adam allows relatively larger steps.

## Learning rate schedules: the “gas pedal” changes over time

Even with Adam, the learning rate often shouldn’t be constant. Common pattern:

- Start larger to make fast progress.
- Decay later to settle into a good minimum.

A simple schedule is step decay (drop LR every N epochs), but cosine decay or warmup+decay are also common in modern training.

## Weight decay: regularization that interacts with optimizers

**Weight decay** discourages large weights (a form of L2 regularization). With adaptive optimizers, it’s often better to use **decoupled weight decay** (commonly called AdamW-style), where decay is applied directly to parameters rather than being mixed into the gradient statistics.

## Minimal PyTorch example

```python
import torch

model = ...

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=1e-3,
    betas=(0.9, 0.999),
    eps=1e-8,
    weight_decay=1e-2,          # consider decoupled_weight_decay=True for AdamW behavior
    decoupled_weight_decay=True,
)

scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.StepLR(optimizer, step_size=10, gamma=0.1)

for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for x, y in loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(x), y)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()
```

## Summary

SGD is the core idea—follow the gradient—but momentum and Adam make that process far more stable and efficient under noisy minibatch gradients. Adam’s key trick is adapting the step size per parameter using running estimates of gradient mean and scale, and learning-rate schedules plus weight decay often matter as much as the optimizer choice.

## Recommended Reading
- [An overview of gradient descent optimization algorithms](https://www.ruder.io/optimizing-gradient-descent/) — Clear intuitions for SGD variants, momentum, and Adam.
- [Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.6980) — The original Adam paper and its motivation.
- [torch.optim.Adam documentation](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.optim.Adam.html) — Practical parameter meanings (betas, eps, weight decay, decoupled decay).
lessons.summary: Modern optimizers improve on SGD by smoothing noisy gradients (momentum) and adapting learning rates per parameter (Adam). Learning-rate schedules and weight decay are crucial companions that often determine whether training is fast and stable.

image_metadata.caption: The Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer lesson as an orientation map; return to it each time a new sub-component (attention, FFN, positional encoding) is introduced so students can locate it in the full pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer model architecture diagram showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, and positional encoding, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, and feed-forward layers, connected by residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled Dot-Product Attention: Q·K scaled, softmaxed, then applied to V.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention formula; use it to walk through each arithmetic step before presenting the matrix equation, and again when explaining why scaling prevents vanishing gradients in softmax.
image_metadata.description: Scaled Dot-Product Attention diagram showing the sequential operations: MatMul of Q and K, scaling by 1/sqrt(d_k), optional masking, softmax, then MatMul with V to produce output.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-Head Attention: h parallel attention heads concatenated and projected.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after explaining single-head scaled dot-product attention; use it to motivate the multi-head design and explain the role of the linear layers before and after the parallel heads.
image_metadata.description: Multi-Head Attention diagram showing h parallel scaled dot-product attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear projection, with Q, K, V inputs each going through separate linear layers per head.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance dependency for 'making' (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the advantages of self-attention over recurrent models for capturing long-distance dependencies, or when motivating why the Transformer was designed without recurrence.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing colored lines from the word 'making' in an encoder self-attention layer (layer 5 of 6) connecting to distant tokens, illustrating how the model captures the long-range dependency 'making…more difficult'. Different colors represent different attention heads.

image_metadata.caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 involved in anaphora resolution (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the isolated 'its' attention panel (Image 6) to contrast full vs. focused attention patterns; use when teaching how different heads learn different linguistic functions.
image_metadata.description: Top panel of anaphora resolution visualization: full attention pattern for head 5 in encoder layer 5 of 6, showing broad attention weights across all tokens in the sentence.

image_metadata.caption: Sharp attention from 'its' resolving anaphora in heads 5 and 6 (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Image 5 to highlight the contrast between broad and sharp attention; use when explaining how attention heads can specialize and what 'sharp' attention distributions mean for model interpretability.
image_metadata.description: Bottom panel of anaphora resolution visualization: isolated attention lines from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to the antecedent noun, demonstrating coreference resolution behavior.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence-structural patterns (layer 5, head A).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing what attention heads learn and model interpretability; pair with Image 8 to illustrate that different heads learn complementary structural patterns.
image_metadata.description: First of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing attention patterns that appear to reflect syntactic sentence structure, with one head attending in a pattern consistent with a specific grammatical relationship.

image_metadata.caption: A second encoder self-attention head learning different structural patterns (layer 5, head B).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 7 as a direct comparison; use to reinforce why multiple heads are beneficial and to discuss how the model distributes different linguistic tasks across heads.
image_metadata.description: Second of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing a different attention pattern from the first head, demonstrating that distinct heads specialize in different structural or semantic tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Why √dₖ? Scaling prevents softmax saturation as dimension grows. (Outcome School)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this after introducing the raw dot-product attention formula Q·Kᵀ and before presenting the full scaled attention formula softmax(Q·Kᵀ/√dₖ)·V — use it to motivate WHY scaling is needed, not just that it exists.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical diagram illustrating the derivation and justification of the √dₖ scaling factor used in scaled dot-product attention, likely showing variance analysis of dot products as dimensionality dₖ grows and how dividing by √dₖ stabilizes the variance to 1.

image_metadata.description: Flowchart illustrating a human-in-the-loop pipeline: GPT-3 generates labeled synthetic data via few-shot prompting, uncertain samples are selected by active learning, sent to human annotators for relabeling, and the refined dataset is used to train a classifier.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-3 labeling wins at low cost but lags behind human labels at scale. (Wang et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the GPT-3 pipeline diagram to quantify its practical benefit; useful when discussing when to use synthetic vs. human labels.

image_metadata.caption: UDG with NLA approaches supervised fine-tuning accuracy on several benchmarks. (Wang et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining NLA to demonstrate its incremental benefit; use to discuss the gap between synthetic-data-trained and fully supervised models.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing robust architecture approaches to noisy labels; use panel (b) to explain why naive end-to-end training of Q fails and why the identity initialization with delayed updates is critical.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: (a) neural network architecture with a noise matrix Q inserted between the softmax layer and the loss function to model label corruption; (b) training schedule showing Q initialized as identity matrix and gradually updated with regularization after initial training epochs.

image_metadata.caption: GLC algorithm: estimating noise transition matrix with trusted data. (Hendrycks et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the noise transition matrix formula, when students need to see how the abstract matrix estimation translates into a concrete training algorithm with clean and noisy data splits.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode/algorithm diagram for Gold Loss Correction (GLC) showing how a noise transition matrix C is estimated using a trusted clean dataset, with steps for computing per-class conditional probabilities of noisy labels given true labels.

image_metadata.caption: L2R bi-level optimization: second-order backprop updates sample weights. (Ren et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why L2R is computationally expensive and how sample weights are derived from validation gradients, especially to clarify the nested optimization loop structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the second-order automatic differentiation (backpropagation through optimization) used in Learning to Reweight (L2R), showing the nested loop: inner loop updates model parameters θ using weighted training loss, outer loop updates sample weights w using validation loss gradient.

image_metadata.caption: L2R results: handles class imbalance (MNIST) and label noise (CIFAR-10). (Ren et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the L2R algorithm to ground the theory in empirical results, particularly when discussing how many clean samples are needed and whether the method handles both noise and imbalance.
image_metadata.description: Two experimental result plots for L2R: left panel shows class imbalance experiment on MNIST (classes 4 vs 9) comparing L2R against baselines; right panel shows CIFAR-10 accuracy vs. number of clean validation samples with 40% label noise flipped to class 3, comparing L2R to baselines.

image_metadata.caption: MentorNet predicts sample weights for StudentNet's weighted training loss. (Jiang et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MentorNet to contrast with L2R — use it to highlight that MentorNet uses a learned network (not gradient-based meta-learning) to produce weights, and to explain the LSTM's role in capturing training dynamics.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of MentorNet and StudentNet trained simultaneously: MentorNet (with LSTM layer) takes per-sample features (loss, loss variance, label, epoch) and outputs scalar sample weights w_i; StudentNet uses these weights in its weighted cross-entropy loss to train on noisy data.

image_metadata.caption: Co-teaching: two networks select low-loss samples to train each other. (Han et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting Co-teaching with MentorNet to highlight the architectural difference (two equal peers vs. mentor-student), and to explain how the small-loss criterion operationalizes the assumption that noisy samples have higher loss.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode algorithm for Co-teaching showing two parallel networks f1 and f2: each mini-batch, both networks select their R(T)% smallest-loss samples and feed those selected samples to the other network for parameter updates, with R(T) increasing over training epochs.

lessons.title: Regularization & Normalization
lessons.content:
# Regularization & Normalization

Training a neural network is a bit like giving a student unlimited time on a take-home exam. If the student is clever enough, they can *memorize* the answer key instead of learning the subject. **Regularization** is how we prevent that memorization. **Normalization** is how we make the learning process itself less chaotic—so gradients don’t explode, vanish, or just wobble around.

This lesson is about both: how to make models generalize *and* how to make training stable.

---

## Regularization: “Make it hard to cheat”

Overfitting happens when the model has enough flexibility to fit quirks of the training set. Regularization adds constraints or noise so the model is pushed toward simpler, more robust solutions.

### **Weight decay (L2 regularization)**
**Weight decay** adds a penalty proportional to the squared weight magnitudes. Intuition: large weights create sharp, brittle decision boundaries; shrinking weights encourages smoother functions.

In practice (PyTorch-style), you usually enable it in the optimizer:

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4, weight_decay=0.01)
```

### **Dropout**
**Dropout** randomly “turns off” a fraction of activations during training. This prevents neurons from relying on very specific partners (reducing *co-adaptation*). A helpful mental model: you’re training an implicit ensemble of many “thinned” networks, and at test time you use the full network as an efficient approximation to averaging them.

![Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training to reduce overfitting (Adapted from CS231n).](/api/wiki-images/neural-networks/images/cs231n-neural-networks-2_004.jpeg)

Typical usage:

```python
x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training)
```

### **Data augmentation**
**Data augmentation** regularizes by expanding the training distribution: random crops, flips, color jitter for images; noise, time masking for audio; token masking or span corruption for text. You’re telling the model: “these variations shouldn’t change the label.”

---

## Normalization: “Make training well-behaved”

Deep nets are fragile partly because each layer’s activations can drift in scale as weights change. Normalization layers actively control these statistics, which tends to stabilize gradients and speed up training.

### **Batch normalization (BatchNorm)**
**BatchNorm** normalizes activations using the mini-batch mean/variance, then learns a scale and shift. It often makes optimization dramatically easier, but it depends on batch statistics—so behavior differs between training and inference (running averages are used at test time).

### **Layer normalization (LayerNorm)**
**LayerNorm** normalizes across features *within a single example* (not across the batch). This makes it batch-size agnostic and a natural fit for sequence models like Transformers.

![Residual connection and layer normalization in a Transformer encoder layer (Adapted from Alammar).](/api/wiki-images/transformer-architecture/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_026.png)

A subtle but important side effect: normalization tends to keep vector norms bounded. That’s one reason dot products inside attention stay in a reasonable range, especially when combined with scaling.

---

## Quick comparison (when to use what)

| Technique | Primary goal | Common gotcha |
|---|---|---|
| weight decay | reduce overfitting via smaller weights | too much can underfit |
| dropout | reduce co-adaptation; implicit ensemble | often less helpful in modern Transformers than in MLPs |
| data augmentation | improve invariances; expand data support | must preserve label semantics |
| batchnorm | stabilize/accelerate training | batch-size dependence; train/test mismatch |
| layernorm | stabilize training without batch dependence | can interact with initialization/learning rate |

*Footnote: Table synthesized from standard deep learning practice; image attributions provided above.*

---

## Summary

**Regularization** fights overfitting by limiting effective model complexity (weight decay), injecting noise (dropout), or expanding the dataset (augmentation). **Normalization** (BatchNorm/LayerNorm) makes optimization smoother by controlling activation statistics, which is especially crucial in deep networks and Transformer-style blocks.

## Recommended Reading
- [CS231n: Regularization & Batch Normalization](https://cs231n.github.io/neural-networks-2/#reg) — Clear intuitions and practical notes on L2 and dropout, plus BatchNorm.
- [Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting](https://jmlr.org/papers/v15/srivastava14a.html) — The original dropout framing as training many thinned networks.
- [nanoGPT (codebase)](https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT) — A compact modern training loop where you can spot weight decay and LayerNorm in the wild.
- [Attention Scoring Functions (D2L)](https://d2l.ai/chapter_attention-mechanisms-and-transformers/attention-scoring-functions.html) — Helpful intuition for why keeping norms controlled matters in attention.
lessons.summary: Regularization reduces overfitting by constraining or perturbing learning (weight decay, dropout, augmentation), while normalization stabilizes training by controlling activation statistics (BatchNorm, LayerNorm). Together they help models generalize better and train more reliably.
lessons.video_title: But what is a neural network?

image_metadata.caption: The Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer lesson as an orientation map; return to it each time a new sub-component (attention, FFN, positional encoding) is introduced so students can locate it in the full pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer model architecture diagram showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, and positional encoding, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, and feed-forward layers, connected by residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training to reduce overfitting. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing dropout as a regularization technique, especially after covering L1/L2 regularization, to contrast structural regularization approaches with weight-penalty approaches.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagram of a neural network without dropout (left, all connections active) versus with dropout (right, some neurons and their connections randomly removed/crossed out), illustrating the dropout regularization technique.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residuals. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder architecture; use to walk through each sub-layer and the role of residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection and layer normalization in a Transformer encoder layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining encoder sub-layer structure; use to show why residual connections matter for training deep networks and how they are implemented in the Transformer.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection and layer normalization step. The input vector is shown bypassing the self-attention sub-layer and being added back to the sub-layer's output before layer norm is applied.

image_metadata.caption: 2-layer Transformer stack with residual connections and layer norm shown. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual encoder residuals; use to generalize the pattern to the full encoder-decoder stack and reinforce the modular, repeated structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, each sub-layer showing residual connections and layer normalization. Arrows show data flow through the stack, illustrating how both encoder and decoder stacks apply Add & Norm.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students need a detailed view of the encoder sub-layer; use to trace the exact path of a vector through self-attention, addition, and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram of a single encoder sub-layer showing the self-attention mechanism with residual connection and layer normalization. Input vectors flow into self-attention, the output is added to the original input (residual), then layer norm is applied.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder block with self-attention and FFN (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from attention as an operation to the full Transformer layer/stack, or when debugging why residuals/norm are needed for stable training.
image_metadata.description: Transformer encoder architecture diagram: token embeddings combined with positional encodings feed stacked encoder blocks containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, each wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled Dot-Product Attention: Q·K scaled, softmaxed, then applied to V.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention formula; use it to walk through each arithmetic step before presenting the matrix equation, and again when explaining why scaling prevents vanishing gradients in softmax.
image_metadata.description: Scaled Dot-Product Attention diagram showing the sequential operations: MatMul of Q and K, scaling by 1/sqrt(d_k), optional masking, softmax, then MatMul with V to produce output.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-Head Attention: h parallel attention heads concatenated and projected.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after explaining single-head scaled dot-product attention; use it to motivate the multi-head design and explain the role of the linear layers before and after the parallel heads.
image_metadata.description: Multi-Head Attention diagram showing h parallel scaled dot-product attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear projection, with Q, K, V inputs each going through separate linear layers per head.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance dependency for 'making' (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the advantages of self-attention over recurrent models for capturing long-distance dependencies, or when motivating why the Transformer was designed without recurrence.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing colored lines from the word 'making' in an encoder self-attention layer (layer 5 of 6) connecting to distant tokens, illustrating how the model captures the long-range dependency 'making…more difficult'. Different colors represent different attention heads.

image_metadata.caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 involved in anaphora resolution (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the isolated 'its' attention panel (Image 6) to contrast full vs. focused attention patterns; use when teaching how different heads learn different linguistic functions.
image_metadata.description: Top panel of anaphora resolution visualization: full attention pattern for head 5 in encoder layer 5 of 6, showing broad attention weights across all tokens in the sentence.

image_metadata.caption: Sharp attention from 'its' resolving anaphora in heads 5 and 6 (layer 5).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Image 5 to highlight the contrast between broad and sharp attention; use when explaining how attention heads can specialize and what 'sharp' attention distributions mean for model interpretability.
image_metadata.description: Bottom panel of anaphora resolution visualization: isolated attention lines from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to the antecedent noun, demonstrating coreference resolution behavior.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence-structural patterns (layer 5, head A).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing what attention heads learn and model interpretability; pair with Image 8 to illustrate that different heads learn complementary structural patterns.
image_metadata.description: First of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing attention patterns that appear to reflect syntactic sentence structure, with one head attending in a pattern consistent with a specific grammatical relationship.

image_metadata.caption: A second encoder self-attention head learning different structural patterns (layer 5, head B).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 7 as a direct comparison; use to reinforce why multiple heads are beneficial and to discuss how the model distributes different linguistic tasks across heads.
image_metadata.description: Second of two encoder self-attention head visualizations from layer 5 of 6, showing a different attention pattern from the first head, demonstrating that distinct heads specialize in different structural or semantic tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Why √dₖ? Scaling prevents softmax saturation as dimension grows. (Outcome School)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this after introducing the raw dot-product attention formula Q·Kᵀ and before presenting the full scaled attention formula softmax(Q·Kᵀ/√dₖ)·V — use it to motivate WHY scaling is needed, not just that it exists.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical diagram illustrating the derivation and justification of the √dₖ scaling factor used in scaled dot-product attention, likely showing variance analysis of dot products as dimensionality dₖ grows and how dividing by √dₖ stabilizes the variance to 1.

image_metadata.caption: Data preprocessing steps: raw, mean-centered, and PCA-rotated. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing data preprocessing before training neural networks, specifically when explaining why centering data matters and what PCA does geometrically.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data point clouds: original data (left), mean-subtracted/centered data (middle), and PCA-rotated data (right), illustrating the geometric effect of each preprocessing step.

image_metadata.caption: Whitening normalizes variance per dimension; right panel shows noise amplification. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining PCA, when introducing whitening as the next preprocessing step, and when discussing its trade-offs including noise sensitivity.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel visualization showing 2D data clouds: PCA-rotated data (left), whitened data with equal variance in all dimensions (middle), and a noisy whitened version (right), illustrating the effect of whitening and its noise-amplification weakness.

image_metadata.caption: PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students ask how PCA/whitening applies to real image data, or after the abstract 2D visualizations to ground the concepts in a practical computer vision context.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of PCA and whitening applied to CIFAR-10 image patches: showing original image patches, their PCA components (eigenvectors as images), and the resulting whitened patches, demonstrating the transformation on real image data.

image_metadata.caption: Raw 2D dataset: two curves the network must classify. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: At the very start of introducing neural network classification; use as the 'problem statement' before showing any network architecture or transformation
image_metadata.description: A 2D scatter plot showing two classes of data points arranged as two curves on a plane, color-coded to distinguish the two classes

image_metadata.caption: Hidden layers enable non-linear decision boundaries on the same dataset. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing the linear classifier result; use to motivate why hidden layers matter by visually demonstrating the gain in expressive power
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization showing how a neural network with hidden layers classifies every point in the input space, displaying a non-linear decision boundary separating the two curve classes

image_metadata.caption: Example neural network architecture with input, hidden, and output layers. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing the network structure being analyzed; reference back to it whenever discussing what happens 'at each layer' during the transformation visualizations
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a small neural network architecture showing input layer, one or more hidden layers, and output layer with labeled nodes and connections

image_metadata.caption: Data in its raw input representation before any network transformation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the idea that each layer creates a new representation; show this as 'Layer 0' before revealing what the hidden layer does to the same data
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization of the dataset as seen in the input (raw) representation — the original space before any layer transformation — showing the two curve classes

image_metadata.caption: Same data after first hidden layer transformation — a new learned representation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after showing the input-space visualization (Image 4); ask students to identify what changed and why that change helps with linear separability
image_metadata.description: A 2D visualization of the same dataset after transformation by the first hidden layer, showing how the data distribution has changed shape in the new representation space

image_metadata.caption: Continuous application of a tanh layer gradually deforms the data manifold. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how a single layer transforms data; use to bridge from the discrete layer-by-layer view to the topological interpretation of network layers
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing the continuous transformation of 2D data as a tanh neural network layer is gradually applied, visualizing the smooth deformation of the data manifold

image_metadata.caption: Four hidden layers successfully untangle slightly entangled spirals into linearly separable classes. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the continuous layer transformation concept; use as a success case before contrasting with the failure case on more entangled spirals
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network attempting to classify two slightly entangled spirals, visualizing the progressive transformation through hidden layers until the spirals become linearly separable

image_metadata.caption: A low-dimensional network fails to untangle more entangled spirals — topology wins. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Directly after the successful spiral case (Image 7); the contrast drives home that topology imposes hard limits, motivating the formal lower-bound argument on network width
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a multi-layer network failing to classify two more heavily entangled spirals, with the network unable to achieve linear separability despite multiple layers

image_metadata.caption: Topologically challenging dataset: class A surrounded by class B in 2D. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the formal topology and classification section; use to make the abstract set definitions A={x|d(x,0)<1/3} and B concrete before walking through the proof
image_metadata.description: A 2D plot showing the topology-based classification problem: class A as a filled disk centered at the origin and class B as an annular ring surrounding it, illustrating a topologically non-trivial dataset

image_metadata.caption: Two hidden units cannot topologically separate the surrounded dataset — stuck at ~80%. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After presenting the impossibility proof; use as empirical confirmation that the theoretical lower bound on hidden units is real and observable in practice
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing a neural network with only two hidden units attempting and failing to classify the surrounded-disk dataset, getting stuck in an unproductive local minimum

image_metadata.caption: 3D dataset where class A is surrounded by B, preventing hyperplane separation. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the proof that standard layers fail for topologically entangled datasets; use to make the abstract argument about determinants and homeomorphisms concrete.
image_metadata.description: A 3D visualization showing two classes of data points (A and B) where one class surrounds the other in 3D space, illustrating a topological classification problem that cannot be solved by a hyperplane in lower dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Third hidden unit lifts 1D entangled classes into linearly separable 2D space. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the 1D classification problem (A = [-1/3, 1/3], B = two outer intervals) to illustrate how a third hidden unit resolves the topological obstruction.
image_metadata.description: A 1D-to-2D transformation visualization showing how a third hidden unit maps the 1D dataset (intervals A and B on a line) into a 2D representation where the two classes become linearly separable.

image_metadata.caption: Animation of neural network learning to separate 1D topological classes during training. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during discussion of how individual hidden units learn threshold functions (firing when x > -1/2 or x > 1/2); use to connect the mathematical description to the visual learning process.
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF showing the training process of a neural network transforming a 1D dataset into a 2D representation, visualizing how the network learns to map the input intervals A and B into a separable configuration.

image_metadata.caption: Two linked rings: a topological obstruction requiring a 4th dimension to separate. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from the 'one class surrounds another' scenario to more general topological obstructions; use to motivate why neural networks need sufficient dimensionality to untangle manifolds.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of two linked rings (a Hopf link or similar link) in 3D space, representing two interlinked 1D manifolds (classes A and B) that cannot be separated without a 4th dimension.

image_metadata.caption: Linked vs. unlinked tori: neural networks untangle manifolds via ambient isotopy. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how neural network layer transformations correspond to ambient isotopies; use to explain why 3 hidden units suffice to classify an unlink but not a true link.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side or comparative visualization showing a linked pair of tori (topologically entangled) alongside an unlinked configuration, illustrating the concept of an unlink and the role of neural network layers as ambient isotopies.

image_metadata.caption: Tangled manifold illustrating why smooth untangling requires contractive regularization. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the failure modes of neural networks on topologically complex data and the motivation for contractive regularization; connects the 2n+2 dimension theorem to practical training concerns.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of a tangled or knotted manifold, illustrating the geometric complexity that arises when a neural network attempts to separate entangled data manifolds, potentially showing high-derivative regions or near-discontinuities.

image_metadata.caption: Vector field deforming a grid, illustrating a proposed manifold-aware neural network layer. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the proposed vector-field-based layer architecture; use to contrast with standard affine transformations and illustrate how anchor-point interpolation could enable more flexible manifold manipulation.
image_metadata.description: A grid deformation visualization showing a vector field applied to a 2D grid, where arrows indicate the direction and magnitude of spatial deformation, illustrating how a learned vector field can reshape the data manifold.

image_metadata.caption: Bubble deformation from a two-anchor vector field layer formula. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)) to help students connect the mathematical expression to its geometric effect on the data space.
image_metadata.description: A 'bubble' deformation of a grid showing a localized, radially symmetric vector field that pushes or pulls a region of space outward, corresponding to the formula F(x) = (v0*f0(x) + v1*f1(x)) / (1 + f0(x) + f1(x)).

image_metadata.caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus regions vs. low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?'
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Words attend to other words with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, before introducing seq2seq models, to extend the analogy from images to language.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weights visualized between words, showing how one word (e.g., 'eating') attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths indicated by shading or line thickness.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: all source info compressed into one vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the limitations of vanilla seq2seq before attention — ask students 'what happens to early words by the time the decoder finishes?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which the RNN decoder uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax, weighted sum, decoder input.
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism within an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional RNN encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, softmax over attention weights, weighted sum to form context vector, and decoder RNN receiving the context vector at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate what trained attention weights look like in practice — ask students to identify reordering patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates attention weight, revealing which source words the model attends to when generating each target word.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to context words (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — contrast with the sentence-level attention example to highlight that source and target are the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing intra-sentence dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention, or when demonstrating attention in computer vision tasks — ask students which image regions they expect the model to attend to for each word.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating specific caption words, demonstrating soft attention over CNN feature maps.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. attention variants after Bahdanau — use the side-by-side layout to directly compare scope of attention at each decoding step.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in Luong et al.'s model.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts beyond NLP to memory-augmented networks — ask students how the attention vector over memory rows parallels attention over encoder hidden states.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the concept of convolutional shift and before discussing sharpening.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after explaining each individual NTM addressing step, or as an overview before diving into each component.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems where output elements are positions in the input (e.g., sorting, TSP), contrasting with standard seq2seq attention.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder hidden states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer's core attention mechanism; show left panel first for single-head attention, then right panel to explain multi-head extension.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and scaling; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the encoder; highlight the masked attention layer and cross-attention to encoder, contrasting with the encoder's structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, and final linear+softmax output.

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or as an overview at the start of a Transformer lesson to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer for sequential/RL tasks and how SNAIL addresses them by combining convolution and attention.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for selective retrieval), used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution accesses local regions; self-attention accesses the entire image.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When motivating the use of self-attention in vision models (SAGAN); use to contrast local convolutional receptive fields with global attention reach.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local region) versus self-attention (entire image/sequence), illustrating the difference in context access.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: Q/K/V via 1x1 convolutions over image features. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), attention map computation via softmax, and weighted sum to produce self-attention feature maps added back to input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)

image_metadata.description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translated sentence out. (Alammar, 2018)

image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder and decoder architecture, showing six encoder layers stacked vertically on the left and six decoder layers stacked vertically on the right, with arrows indicating data flow between them.

image_metadata.caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward networks in detail.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows showing data flow upward through both layers.

image_metadata.caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting the decoder structure with the encoder, specifically to introduce the cross-attention mechanism that connects encoder and decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and a Feed-Forward Neural Network, with arrows indicating data flow including input from the encoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: Each input word is converted to a 512-dimensional embedding vector. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared for the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through encoder layers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing word embedding conversion: input words (e.g., 'You', 'are', 'welcome') are each converted into 512-dimensional embedding vectors represented as colored bars, which then enter the bottom encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Each word flows its own path through the encoder; self-attention creates cross-path dependencies. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how multiple tokens are processed simultaneously in the encoder, and to set up the distinction between self-attention (has cross-position dependencies) and feed-forward (fully parallel).
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors (one per input word) flowing in parallel through the self-attention layer and then the feed-forward layer of a single encoder, with separate paths for each word position.

image_metadata.caption: A short example sentence entering the encoder, preparing for self-attention walkthrough. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show just before diving into the step-by-step self-attention calculation, to establish the example sentence that will be used throughout the detailed walkthrough.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a short example sentence being processed through the encoder, with vectors for each word position flowing through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, used to set up the detailed self-attention explanation.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: 'it' attends strongly to 'animal' to resolve coreference. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention at a high level, before the mathematical details, to give students an intuitive understanding of what the mechanism achieves.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in action: the word 'it' in a sentence is highlighted, with attention lines connecting it to other words (especially 'animal'), showing which words the model attends to when encoding 'it'.

image_metadata.caption: Each word embedding is projected into Query, Key, and Value vectors (dim=64). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the detailed self-attention explanation, as the first step in the Q/K/V computation walkthrough, before showing how scores are calculated.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors for each input word embedding by multiplying with three learned weight matrices (WQ, WK, WV), showing the dimensional reduction from 512 to 64.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.

image_metadata.caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.

image_metadata.caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.

image_metadata.caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
image_metadata.description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.

image_metadata.caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
