image_metadata.caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)

image_metadata.description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)

image_metadata.description: A word-sequence attention illustration: one query vector is compared (via dot products) against key vectors for each word to produce attention weights (softmax implied), which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding an attended output representation.

lessons.title: CNNs & Representation Learning
lessons.content:
# CNNs & Representation Learning

Images aren’t just long vectors of numbers. They’re *grids* where nearby pixels are related, and the same visual pattern (an edge, a corner, a texture) can appear anywhere. **Convolutional neural networks (CNNs)** bake these facts into the architecture, so the model learns *reusable* detectors and builds them into a hierarchy of meaning.

![ConvNet pipeline: INPUT→CONV→RELU→POOL→FC for CIFAR-10 (Adapted from CS231n)](/api/wiki-images/cnns/images/cs231n-convolutional-networks_002.jpeg)

## 1) Convolution: one small detector, used everywhere
A **convolution** layer learns a small set of filters (kernels), like 3×3 or 5×5 patches. Each filter slides across the image and computes a dot product with the local pixels.

Two big ideas are hiding in that sentence:

- **Local receptive fields**: a neuron doesn’t look at the whole image—just a small neighborhood. That’s perfect for edges and textures.
- **Weight sharing**: the *same* filter is applied at every location. If “vertical edge” is useful in the top-left, it’s also useful in the bottom-right.

The output of applying one filter across all positions is a **feature map**: a 2D grid saying “how much did this pattern appear here?” Stack many filters → you get a 3D activation volume (height × width × channels).

## 2) Feature maps become a hierarchy
Early layers tend to learn simple patterns (edges, corners). Deeper layers combine earlier feature maps into more semantic parts (eyes, wheels), and eventually whole objects. This is **representation learning**: the network invents intermediate features that make the final task easy.

A useful mental model: each layer is a new “coordinate system” for the image. Raw pixels are a bad coordinate system for “dog vs. cat”; mid-level features are much better.

## 3) Pooling: downsample, keep what matters
**Pooling** (often max-pooling) reduces spatial resolution, e.g. 32×32 → 16×16, by summarizing small neighborhoods.

Why do this?
- It makes computation cheaper.
- It adds a bit of **translation tolerance**: if an edge shifts by 1–2 pixels, the pooled result can stay similar.

Pooling isn’t the only way to downsample (strided convolutions are common), but the goal is the same: compress space while keeping salient signals.

## 4) A tiny PyTorch sketch (shapes tell the story)
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

x = torch.randn(8, 3, 32, 32)          # (batch, channels, H, W)

conv = nn.Conv2d(3, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
relu = nn.ReLU()
pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2)

h = pool(relu(conv(x)))
print(h.shape)  # torch.Size([8, 16, 16, 16])
```
One conv layer turned 3 channels into 16 feature maps; pooling halved height/width.

## 5) Residual connections: making depth trainable
As networks got deeper, optimization got harder. **Residual connections** (skip connections) fix a lot of this by letting a block learn a *change* to its input:

\[\text{output} = x + F(x)\]

Instead of forcing the block to learn the entire transformation from scratch, it can learn a residual tweak. Practically, this makes very deep CNNs much easier to train and improves accuracy.

## 6) ImageNet: the proving ground
CNNs became a turning point when large-scale training on **ImageNet** (1.3M images, 1000 classes) showed that deep convolutional architectures could dramatically outperform prior approaches. Architectures with multiple conv layers, pooling, and large fully-connected heads demonstrated that learned features scale with data and compute—and kicked off the modern era of deep vision.

![AlexNet ImageNet results: correct and near-miss classifications (Adapted from Olah; originally Krizhevsky et al.)](/api/wiki-images/cnns/images/colah-posts-2014-07-Conv-Nets-Modular_014.png)

## Summary
CNNs work because they respect image structure: **convolutions** learn local, reusable detectors; **feature maps** stack into hierarchies; **pooling** compresses space while keeping salient signals; and **residual connections** make very deep models trainable. ImageNet-scale results cemented these ideas as the backbone of modern representation learning.

## Recommended Reading
- [Convolutional Neural Networks (CS231n notes)](https://cs231n.github.io/convolutional-networks/) — Detailed reference on conv/pool arithmetic, feature maps, and classic architectures.
- [Conv Nets: A Modular Perspective](https://colah.github.io/posts/2014-07-Conv-Nets-Modular/) — Intuition-first explanation of weight sharing and compositional feature building.
- [ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks](https://papers.nips.cc/paper/2012/hash/c399862d3b9d6b76c8436e924a68c45b-Abstract.html) — The landmark ImageNet result that popularized deep CNNs.
- [Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/1512.03385) — Why skip connections unlock very deep networks.
lessons.summary: CNNs learn powerful visual representations by applying shared local filters to produce feature maps, compressing spatial detail with pooling, and scaling depth with residual connections. ImageNet-era breakthroughs showed these architectural biases turn raw pixels into hierarchical features that generalize.

image_metadata.description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, each sub-layer showing residual connections and layer normalization. Arrows show data flow through the stack, illustrating how both encoder and decoder stacks apply Add & Norm.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students need a detailed view of the encoder sub-layer; use to trace the exact path of a vector through self-attention, addition, and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram of a single encoder sub-layer showing the self-attention mechanism with residual connection and layer normalization. Input vectors flow into self-attention, the output is added to the original input (residual), then layer norm is applied.

image_metadata.caption: Full 2D CNN architecture with two stacked convolutional layers. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a CNN introduction lesson as a motivating overview before breaking down individual components
image_metadata.description: A 2D convolutional neural network architecture diagram showing two stacked convolutional layers applied to a 9x5 input grid, illustrating the full modular CNN pipeline

image_metadata.caption: Naive fully-connected network applied to 9 audio samples. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for convolutional layers — use as the 'before' case before presenting the convolutional alternative
image_metadata.description: A diagram of 9 evenly-spaced 1D audio sample inputs connected to a fully-connected layer, representing the naive baseline approach to audio classification

image_metadata.caption: Neuron group A detecting local features in a 1D audio signal. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why convolutions exploit local structure and translational symmetry in sequential data
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single neuron group A applied to a local window of 1D audio samples, illustrating the concept of local feature detection with a shared filter

image_metadata.caption: 1D convolutional layer A (window=2) feeding into fully-connected layer F. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing the convolutional layer operation — use to explain the sliding window and weight sharing mechanism
image_metadata.description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A with a window of 2 points slides across 9 input samples, feeding into a fully-connected layer F

image_metadata.caption: 1D convolutional layer A with window size 3 over 9 inputs. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the 2-point window example to illustrate how window size is a tunable parameter in convolutional layers
image_metadata.description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A uses a window of 3 points, feeding into a fully-connected layer, showing a slightly larger receptive field

image_metadata.caption: Two stacked 1D convolutional layers A and B with shared weights. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how deep CNNs are built by stacking convolutional layers, emphasizing the hierarchical abstraction benefit
image_metadata.description: A diagram of two stacked 1D convolutional layers: neuron group A feeds into neuron group B, which then connects to a fully-connected layer, illustrating layer composability

image_metadata.caption: 1D CNN with Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture for translation invariance. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing max-pooling — use to explain why pooling is inserted between convolutional layers and what invariance it provides
image_metadata.description: A 1D CNN architecture diagram showing a convolutional layer, followed by a max-pooling layer, followed by another convolutional layer, illustrating the Conv-Pool-Conv pattern

image_metadata.caption: Single 2D convolutional unit A scanning a patch of an image. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from 1D to 2D convolutions — use to make the extension to image data concrete and intuitive
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single 2D convolutional unit showing neuron group A looking at a small 2D patch of an input image grid, illustrating the 2D receptive field concept

image_metadata.caption: 2D convolutional layer A applied to a 9×5 image input. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching 2D CNNs for image data — use to explain how a single convolutional layer processes an entire image via sliding patches
image_metadata.description: A full 2D convolutional layer diagram showing neuron group A sliding over a 9x5 input grid, producing a feature map output connected to a fully-connected layer

image_metadata.caption: Full 2D CNN: Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture on a 9×5 image. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a capstone diagram when summarizing 2D CNN architecture — use to consolidate understanding of all components working together on image data
image_metadata.description: A 2D CNN architecture diagram showing a 9x5 input processed by two stacked 2D convolutional layers with a 2D max-pooling layer in between

image_metadata.caption: Standard conv layer: parallel neurons share input, learn distinct features. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the internal structure of a convolutional layer, specifically to explain what happens inside a single sliding-window application
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the standard convolutional layer module A as a set of parallel neurons all receiving the same input region and computing different features simultaneously

image_metadata.caption: Network-in-Network: stacked neuron layers inside each conv module A. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining standard conv layers, when discussing architectural innovations or the concept of increasing representational power within a local receptive field
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Network-in-Network (NIN) Mlpconv variant of module A, showing multiple stacked layers of neurons inside A rather than a single parallel layer, enabling higher-level feature extraction per region

image_metadata.caption: AlexNet architecture: 5 conv + 3 FC layers across two GPUs. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching landmark CNN architectures or motivating why depth and scale matter; use as a case study after explaining individual layer types
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton 2012) deep convolutional neural network, showing 5 convolutional layers with pooling and 3 fully-connected layers split across two GPU streams

image_metadata.caption: AlexNet first-layer filters: edge detectors and color patterns emerge from training. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining what CNNs actually learn, or when motivating why convolutional architectures are well-suited to visual data; powerful for dispelling the 'black box' misconception
image_metadata.description: Visualization of learned first-layer filters from AlexNet, showing one group of neurons that learned oriented edge detectors (black and white) and another group that learned color/texture detectors (color contrasts and patterns)

image_metadata.caption: 1D conv layer: output y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) via sliding window. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When formalizing the convolution operation mathematically; show alongside the equation y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) to bridge diagram and formula
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a 1D convolutional neural network showing input nodes x_n connected through sliding module A to output nodes y_n, illustrating the formal relationship y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) with the convolution window sliding along the sequence

image_metadata.caption: 2D conv layer: y_{n,m} computed from a 2D patch of inputs. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after the 1D convolution diagram, when generalizing to 2D inputs like images; pair with the 2D index notation equation to reinforce the connection
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a 2D convolutional neural network showing a 5x5 (or similar) input grid connected through a 2D sliding module A to a 2D output grid, illustrating the formal 2D convolution relationship y_{n,m} = A(x_{n,m}, x_{n+1,m}, ...)

image_metadata.caption: Regular fully-connected neural network; scales poorly to image inputs. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing why CNNs are needed — use this to contrast with convolutional layers and motivate local connectivity
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a fully-connected (regular) neural network with multiple hidden layers, showing neurons as circles connected by edges across layers, illustrating the dense connectivity pattern

image_metadata.caption: Depth column: local 2D receptive field, full depth connectivity. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining local connectivity in Conv layers, especially to contrast spatial locality with full depth connectivity
image_metadata.description: 3D diagram of a convolutional layer input volume with a highlighted column of neurons showing local 2D spatial connectivity (receptive field) but full depth connectivity, illustrating the asymmetric connectivity principle

image_metadata.caption: Single neuron: weighted inputs summed and passed through activation. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students need a refresher on the basic neuron computation before explaining how filters in a Conv layer perform the same operation over local patches
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single neuron model showing weighted inputs being summed and passed through an activation function, representing the basic computational unit used in convolutional layers

image_metadata.caption: Stride controls filter step size and output spatial dimensions. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the three hyperparameters (depth, stride, zero-padding) that control output volume size in a Conv layer
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the effect of stride on the spatial arrangement of output neurons, showing how a filter slides across a 1D or 2D input with different stride values, producing different output sizes

image_metadata.caption: Parameter sharing: one filter per depth slice applied across all positions. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CNNs reduce parameter count via weight sharing; use to contrast with the 105M parameter count of a fully-connected first layer
image_metadata.description: Visualization of parameter sharing in a convolutional layer, showing that all neurons in a single depth slice share the same filter weights, with different depth slices using different filters (shown as colored weight maps)

image_metadata.caption: Pooling layer reduces spatial size per depth slice, preserving depth. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Pooling layer to illustrate its effect on volume dimensions before discussing the MAX operation in detail
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a pooling layer operating on a 3D input volume, illustrating how it reduces spatial dimensions (width and height) independently per depth slice while preserving depth

image_metadata.caption: Max pooling: 2×2 filter, stride 2 selects maximum from each region. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the max pooling operation in detail; the numerical example makes the abstract operation concrete and easy to verify by hand
image_metadata.description: Illustration of max pooling operation on a 4x4 input grid divided into 2x2 regions, showing how the maximum value is selected from each region to produce a 2x2 output, with specific numbers as examples

image_metadata.caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus regions vs. low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?'
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Words attend to other words with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, before introducing seq2seq models, to extend the analogy from images to language.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weights visualized between words, showing how one word (e.g., 'eating') attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths indicated by shading or line thickness.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: all source info compressed into one vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the limitations of vanilla seq2seq before attention — ask students 'what happens to early words by the time the decoder finishes?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which the RNN decoder uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax, weighted sum, decoder input.
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism within an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional RNN encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, softmax over attention weights, weighted sum to form context vector, and decoder RNN receiving the context vector at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate what trained attention weights look like in practice — ask students to identify reordering patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates attention weight, revealing which source words the model attends to when generating each target word.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to context words (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — contrast with the sentence-level attention example to highlight that source and target are the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing intra-sentence dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention, or when demonstrating attention in computer vision tasks — ask students which image regions they expect the model to attend to for each word.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating specific caption words, demonstrating soft attention over CNN feature maps.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. attention variants after Bahdanau — use the side-by-side layout to directly compare scope of attention at each decoding step.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in Luong et al.'s model.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts beyond NLP to memory-augmented networks — ask students how the attention vector over memory rows parallels attention over encoder hidden states.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the concept of convolutional shift and before discussing sharpening.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after explaining each individual NTM addressing step, or as an overview before diving into each component.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems where output elements are positions in the input (e.g., sorting, TSP), contrasting with standard seq2seq attention.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder hidden states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer's core attention mechanism; show left panel first for single-head attention, then right panel to explain multi-head extension.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and scaling; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residuals. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder architecture; use to walk through each sub-layer and the role of residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the encoder; highlight the masked attention layer and cross-attention to encoder, contrasting with the encoder's structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, and final linear+softmax output.

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or as an overview at the start of a Transformer lesson to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer for sequential/RL tasks and how SNAIL addresses them by combining convolution and attention.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for selective retrieval), used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution accesses local regions; self-attention accesses the entire image.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When motivating the use of self-attention in vision models (SAGAN); use to contrast local convolutional receptive fields with global attention reach.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local region) versus self-attention (entire image/sequence), illustrating the difference in context access.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: Q/K/V via 1x1 convolutions over image features. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), attention map computation via softmax, and weighted sum to produce self-attention feature maps added back to input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — use it to ask 'which parts of the image do you think the attention map focuses on for a dog's eye?'
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the Transformer as a black box, to transition students from the abstract input/output view to the internal component structure.
image_metadata.description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translated sentence out. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer introduction to motivate the architecture with a real-world task before revealing internal components.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with a source-language sentence as input and a target-language translation as output, labeled with 'Encoders' and 'Decoders' blocks.

image_metadata.caption: Six stacked encoders (left) and six stacked decoders (right) in the Transformer. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining that the Transformer uses multiple identical encoder and decoder layers, to reinforce the stacking concept and the number six from the original paper.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder and decoder architecture, showing six encoder layers stacked vertically on the left and six decoder layers stacked vertically on the right, with arrows indicating data flow between them.

image_metadata.caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward networks in detail.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows showing data flow upward through both layers.

image_metadata.caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting the decoder structure with the encoder, specifically to introduce the cross-attention mechanism that connects encoder and decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and a Feed-Forward Neural Network, with arrows indicating data flow including input from the encoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: Each input word is converted to a 512-dimensional embedding vector. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared for the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through encoder layers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing word embedding conversion: input words (e.g., 'You', 'are', 'welcome') are each converted into 512-dimensional embedding vectors represented as colored bars, which then enter the bottom encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Each word flows its own path through the encoder; self-attention creates cross-path dependencies. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how multiple tokens are processed simultaneously in the encoder, and to set up the distinction between self-attention (has cross-position dependencies) and feed-forward (fully parallel).
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors (one per input word) flowing in parallel through the self-attention layer and then the feed-forward layer of a single encoder, with separate paths for each word position.

image_metadata.caption: A short example sentence entering the encoder, preparing for self-attention walkthrough. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show just before diving into the step-by-step self-attention calculation, to establish the example sentence that will be used throughout the detailed walkthrough.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a short example sentence being processed through the encoder, with vectors for each word position flowing through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, used to set up the detailed self-attention explanation.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: 'it' attends strongly to 'animal' to resolve coreference. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention at a high level, before the mathematical details, to give students an intuitive understanding of what the mechanism achieves.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in action: the word 'it' in a sentence is highlighted, with attention lines connecting it to other words (especially 'animal'), showing which words the model attends to when encoding 'it'.

image_metadata.caption: Each word embedding is projected into Query, Key, and Value vectors (dim=64). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the detailed self-attention explanation, as the first step in the Q/K/V computation walkthrough, before showing how scores are calculated.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors for each input word embedding by multiplying with three learned weight matrices (WQ, WK, WV), showing the dimensional reduction from 512 to 64.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.

image_metadata.caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.

image_metadata.caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.

image_metadata.caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
image_metadata.description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.

image_metadata.caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same input being processed through eight parallel sets of Q/K/V weight matrices, each producing a separate Z matrix (Z0 through Z7).

image_metadata.caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head QKV diagram to pose the 'how do we combine these?' problem before revealing the W^O projection solution.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the eight Z matrices problem, as the resolution step, and emphasize that W^O is also a learned parameter trained end-to-end.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the concatenation of all eight Z matrices followed by multiplication by weight matrix W^O to produce a single combined output matrix, completing the multi-headed attention mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Complete multi-headed self-attention pipeline from input to output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation diagram after explaining each individual step, or use as a reference when students need to review the complete multi-head attention mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-headed self-attention pipeline in one view: input embeddings → multiple Q/K/V projections → parallel attention computations → concatenation → W^O projection → final output.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads reveal which words 'it' attends to during encoding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-headed attention mechanics to demonstrate real-world utility — use it to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial by showing they capture different linguistic phenomena.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing which tokens different attention heads focus on when encoding the word 'it' in the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to other words.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the complexity of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about how different heads specialize.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, making the combined picture cluttered and hard to interpret.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use this before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap to build intuition with a simple 4D example.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder. Each word's embedding is shown as a colored vector, with a corresponding positional encoding vector added to it, illustrated with a 4-dimensional example.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after explaining that positional encodings are added to embeddings; use as a stepping stone before the large 512-dimension heatmap.
image_metadata.description: Small heatmap visualization of positional encoding values for a short sequence with 4-dimensional embeddings. Each row represents a word position and each column a dimension, color-coded to show values between -1 and 1.

image_metadata.caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap — sinusoidal patterns visible. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss how the sinusoidal structure allows generalization to unseen sequence lengths.

lessons.title: RNNs, LSTMs & Sequence Modeling
lessons.content:
# RNNs, LSTMs & Sequence Modeling

A lot of data isn’t a single “thing” — it’s a **story**: characters in a name, words in a sentence, frames in a video, sensor readings over time. The defining feature is order. If you shuffle the elements, you change the meaning.

## 1) The core idea of an RNN: a state that persists
A **recurrent neural network (RNN)** is just a neural net that carries a vector forward through time: the **hidden state**. At each timestep, it reads the current input and updates its state.

![Rolled RNN: module A recurrently passes hidden state h_t. (Olah, 2015)](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/colah-posts-2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs_001.png)

If we “unroll” that loop, you can see the same computation repeated at every step (shared weights):

![Standard RNN repeating module: a single tanh layer per time step. (Olah, 2015)](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/colah-posts-2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs_005.png)

A common vanilla RNN update looks like:

\[
\mathbf{h}_t = g(\mathbf{U}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}_t), \quad \mathbf{y}_t = f(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{h}_t)
\]

Where \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the input at time \(t\), \(\mathbf{h}_t\) is “what we remember so far”, and \(\mathbf{y}_t\) is the output (often a softmax over tokens).

## 2) Autoregressive generation: the RNN as a next-step predictor
In language modeling, the RNN is trained to predict the next token given previous ones. At inference time, you feed its own predictions back in, one step at a time. That’s how a model can generate text character-by-character or word-by-word.

```python
h = zeros(H)
x = BOS
for t in range(max_len):
    h = tanh(U @ h + W @ embed(x))
    logits = V @ h
    x = sample(softmax(logits))
    if x == EOS: break
    emit(x)
```

This “predict next, then feed it back” pattern is the backbone of sequence generation.

## 3) Why vanilla RNNs struggle: long-term dependencies & vanishing gradients
In principle, an RNN *could* remember something from 50 steps ago. In practice, training pushes gradients backward through many repeated multiplications (backpropagation through time). Those gradients often **shrink toward zero** (or explode), making it hard to learn long-range dependencies.

So we want a mechanism that makes “remembering” and “forgetting” easier to learn.

## 4) LSTMs: memory with gates
An **LSTM** adds a second state — the **cell state** \(\mathbf{c}_t\) — and uses **gates** (sigmoids) to control information flow. Think of gates as learned knobs in \([0,1]\) that decide what to keep.

![LSTM chain: four interacting layers per module enable long-term memory. (Olah, 2015)](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/colah-posts-2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs_006.png)

The key update is:

- **Forget gate** decides what to erase from \(\mathbf{c}_{t-1}\)
- **Input gate** decides what new info to write

![LSTM cell state update: forget old info, add new candidate values. (Olah, 2015)](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/colah-posts-2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs_012.png)

Then the **output gate** decides what part of the cell state becomes the hidden state \(\mathbf{h}_t\):

![LSTM output gate: sigmoid filters cell state through tanh to produce h_t. (Olah, 2015)](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/colah-posts-2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs_013.png)

This design gives gradients a “cleaner highway” through time, making long-range learning much more feasible.

## 5) Seq2Seq: when input and output are both sequences
Many tasks aren’t “one label per timestep” — they’re “sequence in, sequence out”: translation, summarization, dialogue.

A classic **sequence-to-sequence (encoder–decoder)** model uses:
- an **encoder RNN** to read the input and produce a final hidden state (a **context vector**)
- a **decoder RNN** to generate the output, conditioned on that context

![Context vector (size 4) bridges encoder and decoder in seq2seq. — Jay Alammar](/api/wiki-images/rnns-lstms/images/jalammar-visualizing-neural-machine-translation-mechanics-of-seq2seq-models-with_001.png)

This works well for short sequences, but it creates a bottleneck: everything must be compressed into one fixed-size vector.

## 6) Two practical upgrades: bidirectionality and attention (motivation)
- **Bidirectional RNNs** run one RNN left→right and another right→left, then combine their states. This helps when “future context” matters (e.g., disambiguating a word using what comes later).
- **Attention** (in seq2seq) lets the decoder look back at *all* encoder states, not just the final one—reducing the fixed-vector bottleneck and improving long-sequence handling.

## Summary
RNNs model sequences by carrying a hidden state forward through time, enabling autoregressive prediction and generation. LSTMs fix the “forgetting over long distances” problem by adding a cell state and gates that learn what to keep, write, and expose—making long-range dependencies trainable and powering classic seq2seq systems.

## Recommended Reading
- [The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks](https://karpathy.github.io/2015/05/21/rnn-effectiveness/) — Intuition for why simple recurrent models can generate surprisingly coherent sequences.
- [Understanding LSTM Networks](https://colah.github.io/posts/2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs/) — The clearest mental model of gates, cell state, and why LSTMs help.
- [CS224n Notes: Neural Machine Translation, Seq2seq and Attention](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs224n/readings/cs224n-2019-notes06-NMT_seq2seq_attention.pdf) — A structured walkthrough of encoder–decoder training and attention motivation.
- [Sequence-to-Sequence Learning for Machine Translation (D2L)](https://d2l.ai/chapter_recurrent-modern/seq2seq.html) — Practical details like teacher forcing and decoding behavior.
lessons.summary: RNNs process sequences by repeatedly updating a hidden state, enabling next-token prediction and generation. LSTMs add gated memory (cell state) to preserve information over long spans, making seq2seq sequence transduction far more effective and motivating attention to avoid fixed-context bottlenecks.

image_metadata.caption: Short-term dependencies: nearby context is easily captured by standard RNNs. (Olah, 2015)

image_metadata.description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating short-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context (shown in darker shading) is only a few steps away from the current prediction point

image_metadata.caption: Long-term dependencies: distant context is hard for standard RNNs to learn. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the short-term dependencies diagram; ask students why the distance between context and prediction matters before revealing the LSTM solution
image_metadata.description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating long-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context is many time steps away from the current prediction, with fading or distant connections indicating difficulty

image_metadata.caption: LSTM notation guide: boxes, circles, and lines explained. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before any detailed LSTM gate diagram; return to it whenever a student seems confused by a symbol in a subsequent diagram
image_metadata.description: A notation legend diagram for LSTM diagrams showing: yellow boxes (neural network layers), pink circles (pointwise operations), single arrows (vector flow), merging lines (concatenation), and forking lines (copy operations)

image_metadata.caption: LSTM cell state: an information highway enabling long-range memory. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first explaining the cell state concept; use the conveyor belt analogy and ask students what advantage a direct path for information provides over the hidden state alone
image_metadata.description: An LSTM diagram highlighting the cell state as a horizontal line running across the top of the network through all time steps, with minimal interactions shown, emphasizing its role as an information highway

image_metadata.caption: LSTM gate: sigmoid layer controls how much information passes through. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing any specific gate (forget/input/output); ensure students can explain what a value of 0 vs 1 means before proceeding to the full LSTM walkthrough
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single LSTM gate showing a sigmoid neural network layer feeding into a pointwise multiplication operation, illustrating how gates control information flow with outputs between 0 and 1

image_metadata.caption: Forget gate: sigmoid decides what to erase from cell state C_{t-1}. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the first step in the LSTM walkthrough after explaining the gate notation; use the 'predicting next word after subject changes' example to ground the abstract mechanism
image_metadata.description: An LSTM diagram with the forget gate highlighted, showing a sigmoid layer taking h_t-1 and x_t as inputs and producing f_t, which is multiplied pointwise with the previous cell state C_t-1

image_metadata.caption: LSTM input gate: sigmoid selects updates, tanh creates candidates C̃_t. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the forget gate; use this to show the second of three LSTM gate operations before combining them in the cell state update.
image_metadata.description: LSTM diagram highlighting the input gate: a sigmoid layer (input gate) and a tanh layer producing candidate values C̃_t, showing how new information is prepared for storage in the cell state.

image_metadata.caption: Peephole LSTM: gate layers receive direct cell state input. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After fully explaining the standard LSTM; introduce when discussing architectural variants and why researchers modify the baseline model.
image_metadata.description: LSTM variant diagram showing peephole connections, where additional lines feed the cell state C_t directly into all three gate sigmoid layers, allowing gates to condition on the current memory value.

image_metadata.caption: Coupled-gate LSTM: forget and input decisions are tied together. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing LSTM variants; use alongside the peephole diagram to show a spectrum of modifications from additive (peepholes) to reductive (coupled gates).
image_metadata.description: LSTM variant diagram showing coupled forget and input gates, where a single decision controls both forgetting and inputting: the input gate is replaced by (1 - f_t), so the network only writes when it forgets.

image_metadata.caption: Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder, not just the last. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the moment of introducing attention as a solution to the context vector bottleneck; contrast directly with the previous context vector diagram to highlight what changes architecturally.
image_metadata.description: Diagram contrasting classic seq2seq with an attention-based model, showing that the encoder passes ALL hidden states (not just the last one) to the decoder, illustrating the key architectural difference that enables attention.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights reveal which input words inform each decoded output word. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention mechanism mathematically or architecturally; use to demonstrate real-world learned alignment behavior and to motivate why attention improves translation of long or reordered sentences.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of attention weights across input sentence words at each decoding step, showing which source words the model focuses on when generating each target word, likely as a heatmap or highlighted sentence alignment for a French-to-English translation example.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq RNN encoder–decoder for machine translation. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing seq2seq models, before any code, to anchor the overall data flow from source to target sequence.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a sequence-to-sequence model showing an RNN encoder processing input tokens (e.g., 'They', 'are', 'watching') into a fixed-shape context vector, which initializes an RNN decoder that generates output tokens (e.g., 'Ils', 'regardent') one at a time, with special BOS/EOS tokens marking sequence boundaries.

image_metadata.caption: Decoder detail: context vector concatenated at each time step. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the decoder implementation, specifically when discussing how encoded information is passed to and maintained throughout the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram of the seq2seq decoder showing how the encoder's final hidden state initializes the decoder, and how the context variable is concatenated with the decoder input at every time step before being fed into the RNN layers, with a fully connected output layer on top.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the training section to the prediction/inference section, to contrast with the training diagram and explain why inference is more complex.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the seq2seq prediction (inference) process: the encoder processes the source sequence into a context vector, then the decoder generates output tokens autoregressively, feeding each predicted token as the next input until an EOS token is produced, without teacher forcing.

image_metadata.caption: Visual attention: high-res focus on key regions, low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?' to prime the concept.
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Each word 'attends' to others with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the visual attention analogy and before introducing the mathematical definition of attention, to transition from images to text-based attention.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weight visualizations showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence, with varying highlight intensities indicating attention strength between word pairs.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one context vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention — ask students 'what happens to early words when the sentence is very long?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder hidden states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax weights, weighted sum — before presenting the equations.
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, context vector as a weighted sum, and the decoder producing output tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English word correspondences learned automatically. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate that the model learns interpretable alignments — use the off-diagonal cells to discuss language reordering.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell intensity shows the attention weight between each source-target word pair, revealing mostly diagonal alignment with some cross-language reordering.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention — use the overlay to explain that soft attention is differentiable (weighted sum) while hard attention would select exactly one region.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating each word of the caption.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering Bahdanau attention and soft/hard distinction — use the diagrams to compare architectures side by side and discuss when local attention is preferable for long sequences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing Luong's global attention (left, attending over all source positions) and local attention (right, attending over a window of source positions centered on a predicted alignment point), both showing the decoder hidden state, attention layer, and context vector computation.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller reads/writes memory via soft attention. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts to memory-augmented networks — draw the parallel between NTM memory addressing and encoder-decoder attention weights explicitly.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) producing attention weight vectors over memory rows.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of shift weighting distribution s_t in NTM addressing.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the interpolation gate and before discussing the sharpening step, to clarify how shift distributions are constructed.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content lookup → interpolation → shift → sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after introducing each individual NTM addressing component, or as an overview at the start of the NTM addressing section to give students a roadmap.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the NTM addressing mechanism showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing → interpolation with previous weights → location-based shift convolution → sharpening, producing the final attention vector w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions as output. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks, show this after explaining the limitation of standard seq-2-seq models with fixed output vocabularies, to visually demonstrate how attention is repurposed as a pointer.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and linear projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer's core attention mechanism; use the left panel first to explain single-head attention, then the right panel to explain parallelism and concatenation.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers stacked N times. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining multi-head attention in isolation, show this to demonstrate how it fits into the full encoder stack with residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and encoder cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the encoder, show this to highlight the two key differences in the decoder: masking and cross-attention, and discuss why each is necessary for sequence generation.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward network, residual connections, layer norm, and final linear+softmax output layer.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL combines causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning tasks. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer's positional encoding for reinforcement learning or meta-learning, show this as an example of a hybrid architecture that compensates with explicit temporal convolutions.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and causal attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information) used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention accesses all spatial positions globally.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or comparing CNNs to attention-based models, show this early to build intuition for why convolutions struggle with global context before explaining the self-attention solution.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the limited local receptive field of a convolutional operation (small highlighted region) versus the global receptive field of self-attention (entire image accessible), illustrating the spatial coverage difference.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features split into key, query, value via 1×1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Transformer's attention mechanism, show this to demonstrate how the same KQV framework applies to image feature maps, reinforcing the generality of the attention concept across domains.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing convolutional feature maps branched into three paths (f, g, h corresponding to key, query, value), attention map computation via softmax, weighted sum to produce attended features, and residual addition to input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showcasing self-attention quality. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention mechanism and the attention map formula, to give students a tangible payoff — use it to ask: 'What visual properties (e.g., fur texture consistency, symmetry) might require long-range attention to generate correctly?'
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals, demonstrating the visual quality and diversity of outputs achievable with self-attention mechanisms in generative adversarial networks.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla seq2seq uses a single context vector for decoding (Dive into Deep Learning).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right before introducing attention, when students ask why long sentences degrade translation quality.
image_metadata.description: A classic RNN encoder–decoder (seq2seq) diagram: source tokens are encoded into a fixed-dimensional context/state, which is then passed to an RNN decoder that generates target tokens step by step.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau attention in an RNN encoder–decoder (Dive into Deep Learning).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how attention replaces a single fixed context with a time-varying context per output token.
image_metadata.description: A detailed seq2seq-with-attention architecture: encoder hidden states across all time steps feed an additive (Bahdanau) attention module; at each decoder step, the previous decoder hidden state forms the query, producing a context vector that is combined with the current input embedding to update the decoder state and predict the next token.

lessons.title: Word Embeddings & Representation
lessons.content:
# Word Embeddings & Representation

Neural networks don’t “understand” words—they understand numbers. The trick is to choose numbers that *carry meaning*, so that learning about **awesome** helps the model also handle **great**.

![A word represented as a dense numeric vector (embedding). — Adapted from Alammar](/api/wiki-images/word-embeddings/images/jalammar-illustrated-bert_011.png)

## From IDs and one-hot vectors to meaning
A naive approach is to map each word to an arbitrary ID (e.g., `awesome → 5321`). But IDs have no geometry: 5321 isn’t “closer” to 5319 in any meaningful way.

A slightly less naive approach is **one-hot encoding**: a vocabulary-sized vector with a single 1. It’s unambiguous, but it’s huge and still doesn’t express similarity.

What we want instead is a **word embedding**: a short, dense vector (say 100–1000 numbers) where distance and direction encode useful relationships.

## The distributional hypothesis (why embeddings work)
The core intuition is the **distributional hypothesis**: *words that appear in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings.*

If “kitten” often appears near “cute”, “pet”, “cat”, and “adopt”, then its vector should end up near “puppy” (which appears near “cute”, “pet”, “dog”, “adopt”).

## Word2Vec: learn vectors by predicting neighbors
**Word2Vec** turns that intuition into a training game.

### Skip-gram (target → context)
Pick a target word, and try to predict the words around it (within a sliding window). Training creates many (target, context) pairs.

![Skip-gram model: one-hot input → N-dim embedding → softmax output. — Adapted from Weng](/api/wiki-images/word-embeddings/images/lilianweng-posts-2017-10-15-word-embedding_001.png)

The embedding is not a handcrafted feature vector—it’s literally the weights the model learns for each word.

### CBOW (context → target)
Flip the direction: average the surrounding word vectors and predict the center word.

![CBOW model: multiple context vectors averaged → predict target word. — Adapted from Weng](/api/wiki-images/word-embeddings/images/lilianweng-posts-2017-10-15-word-embedding_002.png)

In practice, Word2Vec is trained efficiently with tricks like **negative sampling** (teach the model what *isn’t* a plausible neighbor) and subsampling very frequent words.

## GloVe: learn vectors from global co-occurrence counts
Word2Vec learns from local prediction tasks. **GloVe** takes a more “global statistics” view: build a big matrix of how often words co-occur, then factor it into dense vectors. Different route, similar destination: vectors where similarity and analogies often emerge.

## Static vs contextual embeddings
Classic Word2Vec/GloVe give each word **one fixed vector**. That’s a problem for polysemy:

- “stick” in *"a stick in the mud"* vs *"stick the landing"*.

![GloVe gives 'stick' one fixed vector regardless of meaning. — Adapted from Alammar](/api/wiki-images/word-embeddings/images/jalammar-illustrated-bert_013.png)

**Contextual embeddings** (ELMo, BERT, modern transformers) produce a different vector for the same token depending on surrounding words.

![ELMo assigns different embeddings to 'stick' based on context. — Adapted from Alammar](/api/wiki-images/word-embeddings/images/jalammar-illustrated-bert_012.png)

## A tiny code sketch: embeddings as a lookup table
At implementation time, an embedding layer is basically a learned matrix `E` of shape `(vocab_size, d)`.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

vocab_size = 10000
embed_dim = 256

E = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)

word_ids = torch.tensor([42, 7, 999])   # e.g., ["kitten", "is", "cute"]
vectors = E(word_ids)                  # (3, 256)
```

Training decides where each row vector should live so that the model’s language task becomes easy.

## Summary
Word embeddings replace sparse, arbitrary word representations with dense vectors where geometry reflects meaning. Word2Vec learns these vectors via local prediction (skip-gram/CBOW), GloVe learns them from global co-occurrence statistics, and contextual models go further by making embeddings depend on the surrounding text.

## Recommended Reading
- [The Illustrated Word2vec](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-word2vec/) — Intuitive, visual walkthrough of skip-gram, windows, and negative sampling.
- [Learning Word Embedding](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2017-10-15-word-embedding/) — Clear technical reference covering Word2Vec and GloVe.
- [The Illustrated BERT, ELMo, and co.](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-bert/) — Great bridge from static embeddings to contextual representations.
lessons.summary: Word embeddings map words to dense vectors where similarity in vector space corresponds to similarity in meaning. Word2Vec and GloVe learn static embeddings from context/co-occurrence, while contextual models produce different embeddings for the same word depending on its sentence.

image_metadata.caption: GloVe assigns fixed vectors to words, ignoring context. (Alammar)

image_metadata.caption: A word represented as a dense numeric vector (embedding). — Jay Alammar
