image_metadata.caption: ELMo assigns different embeddings to 'stick' based on context. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of polysemy and the motivation for contextualized embeddings, after students understand static embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram contrasting a static GloVe embedding for the word 'stick' with ELMo's context-sensitive embedding, illustrating that ELMo assigns different vectors to the same word depending on surrounding context.

image_metadata.caption: GloVe gives 'stick' one fixed vector regardless of meaning. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitation of fixed embeddings, just before introducing ELMo and contextualized representations.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of a GloVe word embedding for the word 'stick' as a long vector of numbers, demonstrating the fixed, context-independent nature of static word embeddings.

image_metadata.caption: Skip-gram model: one-hot input → N-dim embedding → softmax output. (Weng, 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the skip-gram model, specifically when explaining how a single target word is used to predict a context word and how the hidden layer encodes the word embedding of size N.
image_metadata.description: Neural network architecture diagram of the skip-gram model showing a one-hot encoded input vector x of size V, a hidden layer of size N representing the word embedding, weight matrices W (V×N) and W' (N×V), and a softmax output layer producing a one-hot encoded output vector y of size V.

image_metadata.caption: CBOW model: multiple context vectors averaged → predict target word. (Weng, 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the skip-gram diagram when comparing the two word2vec architectures, emphasizing the reversed prediction direction (context → target) and the averaging of multiple input vectors in the hidden layer.
image_metadata.description: Neural network architecture diagram of the CBOW model showing multiple one-hot encoded context word input vectors, individual projections through weight matrix W into N-dimensional vectors, an averaging operation in the hidden layer to produce a single fixed-length vector, and a softmax output layer predicting the target word.

image_metadata.caption: Transfer learning transforms NLP, mirroring ImageNet's impact on vision. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on BERT or NLP transfer learning to motivate why these models matter and situate them historically.
image_metadata.description: A timeline or overview diagram showing the transfer learning paradigm in NLP, illustrating how pre-trained models (like BERT, ELMo, ULMFiT) represent an inflection point in NLP, analogous to ImageNet's impact on computer vision.

image_metadata.caption: BERT fine-tuned for spam classification with a classifier head. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how BERT is applied to downstream tasks, before diving into architecture details, to ground students in practical usage.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing BERT used for spam classification: input text flows into the BERT model, and a classifier head on top outputs a spam/not-spam prediction.

image_metadata.caption: BERT Base vs. BERT Large: encoder layers, hidden units, attention heads. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing BERT's architecture section to help students distinguish the two model sizes and understand the role of hyperparameters.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of BERT Base and BERT Large model sizes, showing differences in number of encoder layers, hidden units, and attention heads.

image_metadata.caption: BERT's stacked encoder blocks: 12 (Base) vs. 24 (Large) layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the model architecture explanation to help students visualize the stacking of encoder blocks and compare depths between BERT variants.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the stacked encoder layers inside BERT Base (12 layers) and BERT Large (24 layers), visualizing the depth of the Transformer encoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: BERT input sequence with [CLS] token produces per-token output vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's input requirements and output structure, especially before discussing how the [CLS] output is used for classification.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing BERT's input-output structure: a sequence of word tokens (with [CLS] as the first token) fed into the encoder stack, producing a corresponding sequence of output vectors.

image_metadata.caption: Tokens flow through BERT's encoder layers via self-attention and feed-forward networks. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal mechanics of BERT's encoder stack to illustrate how information flows and transforms through the network.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating how input tokens flow upward through BERT's stacked encoder layers, with each layer applying self-attention and feed-forward operations before passing results to the next layer.

image_metadata.caption: BERT's [CLS] output: a 768-dim vector fed to the classifier. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how BERT's output is connected to a classifier, particularly to clarify why only the [CLS] position output is used for sentence-level tasks.
image_metadata.description: A diagram highlighting the output vector at the [CLS] position (first token) from BERT's final encoder layer, showing it as a 768-dimensional vector used as input to a downstream classifier.

image_metadata.caption: A simple single-layer classifier on BERT's output achieves strong results. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing fine-tuning and the classifier architecture to emphasize how lightweight the task-specific component is relative to the pre-trained BERT model.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single-layer neural network classifier receiving the [CLS] output vector from BERT and producing classification probabilities (e.g., spam/not spam) via softmax.

image_metadata.caption: VGGNet's conv+classifier mirrors BERT's encoder+head structure. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when drawing the parallel between BERT and CNNs to help students with vision backgrounds understand the transfer learning paradigm by analogy.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of VGGNet architecture showing the convolutional feature extraction layers followed by fully-connected classification layers, used as an analogy to BERT + classifier head.

image_metadata.caption: ELMo's bi-directional LSTM hidden states power its embeddings. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining ELMo's pre-training mechanism and how hidden states from an LSTM become the basis for contextualized embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of ELMo's bi-directional LSTM architecture performing language modeling, showing hidden states emerging from each LSTM step and being used to form embeddings.

image_metadata.caption: ELMo trains forward and backward LSTMs simultaneously for context. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the difference between unidirectional and bidirectional language models, and how ELMo's training objective differs from GPT.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of ELMo's forward and backward language model pre-training, showing the input sequence 'Let's stick to' with the model predicting the next word in both directions.

lessons.title: The Attention Breakthrough
lessons.content:
# The Attention Breakthrough

Seq2seq used to have a very “tight pipe” between encoder and decoder: the encoder reads the whole source sentence and squeezes everything into **one fixed-length vector**. Then the decoder tries to generate a whole translation from that single summary.

That works… until sentences get long or syntax gets twisty. The model is forced to store *every detail it might need later* in a single hidden state. This is the **seq2seq bottleneck**.

Bahdanau attention is the moment we loosened that pipe.

![Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder, not just the last. — Jay Alammar (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/attention-mechanism/images/jalammar-visualizing-neural-machine-translation-mechanics-of-seq2seq-models-with_003.png)

## 1) The key idea: a different context vector at every decoding step

Instead of giving the decoder one static context vector `c`, attention gives it a **dynamic context vector** `c_t` that changes with time step `t`.

Intuition: when you’re about to generate the French word for “cat”, you want to look at the part of the English sentence that talks about the cat—not the whole sentence equally.

So at each decoder step `t`:

- the decoder has a hidden state **sₜ** (what it currently “needs”)
- the encoder has hidden states **h₁ … h_T** (what the source “contains” at each position)
- attention computes weights over all encoder states, then mixes them into **cₜ**

## 2) Alignment scores: “how relevant is source position i right now?”

Attention starts by computing an **alignment score** (also called a compatibility score) between the current decoder state `s_t` and each encoder state `h_i`:

\[
 e_{t,i} = \text{score}(s_t, h_i)
\]

Bahdanau’s specific choice is **additive attention**: a small neural network that learns how to compare `s_t` and `h_i`.

Then we normalize these scores into a probability distribution with softmax:

\[
\alpha_{t,i} = \text{softmax}(e_{t,i})
\]

Now `α_{t,i}` literally means: *at decoding step t, how much attention do we pay to source token i?*

## 3) The context vector is a weighted sum (soft lookup)

Once we have weights, we compute the context vector:

\[
 c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i
\]

This is the “soft lookup” view: the decoder queries the encoder’s memory and gets back a blend of the most relevant pieces.

![Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder hidden states. — Lilian Weng (Adapted from Weng)](/api/wiki-images/attention-mechanism/images/lilianweng-posts-2018-06-24-attention_004.png)

## 4) Why this breaks the bottleneck

In vanilla seq2seq, the decoder can only access the source through one vector. With attention, the decoder gets:

- **direct access to all encoder states** at every step
- a learned, differentiable way to choose what matters *right now*

So long sentences stop being “compress everything perfectly or fail.” The model can keep rich, position-specific information in `h_i` and retrieve it when needed.

A nice side effect: the attention weights often form an interpretable **alignment matrix** between source and target tokens.

![Attention alignment matrix: learned word correspondences. — Lilian Weng (Adapted from Weng)](/api/wiki-images/attention-mechanism/images/lilianweng-posts-2018-06-24-attention_005.png)

## 5) Minimal pseudocode (one decoding step)

```python
# encoder_states: [T, d]  (h_1 ... h_T)
# decoder_state:  [d]     (s_t)

scores = [MLP(decoder_state, h_i) for h_i in encoder_states]  # e_{t,i}
alpha  = softmax(scores)                                     # α_{t,i}
context = sum(a * h for a, h in zip(alpha, encoder_states))  # c_t

# decoder uses context to update state / produce next token
next_decoder_state = RNN(decoder_state, prev_token, context)
```

This is the core mechanism that later gets re-expressed in Transformers as query/key/value attention—same spirit, different packaging.

## Summary

Bahdanau attention removes the seq2seq bottleneck by replacing a single fixed context vector with a per-step context `c_t` computed as a soft, learned lookup over all encoder states. The heart of the method is alignment scores → softmax weights → weighted sum, a template that became the foundation for modern attention-based models.

## Recommended Reading

- [11.4. The Bahdanau Attention Mechanism](https://d2l.ai/chapter_attention-mechanisms-and-transformers/bahdanau-attention.html) — Clear walkthrough of the bottleneck and the additive attention computation.
- [CS224n Notes: Neural Machine Translation, Seq2seq and Attention](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs224n/readings/cs224n-2019-notes06-NMT_seq2seq_attention.pdf) — Solid conceptual + mathematical framing of encoder-decoder attention.
- [The Illustrated Transformer](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/) — Great bridge from encoder-decoder attention to Transformer attention.
- [Attention and Self-Attention for NLP (LMU Seminar)](https://slds-lmu.github.io/seminar_nlp_ss20/attention-and-self-attention-for-nlp.html) — Helpful narrative comparison of vanilla seq2seq vs attention.
lessons.summary: Bahdanau attention solved the seq2seq bottleneck by letting the decoder compute a fresh context vector at each step as a weighted sum over all encoder states. Those weights come from learned alignment scores, turning “translation” into “translate while softly aligning.”

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as part of a side-by-side comparison of all training curves when emphasizing that the mathematical model is framework-independent.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve for the seq2seq model from a fourth framework variant (e.g., TensorFlow), showing loss decreasing over training steps.

image_metadata.caption: Autoregressive seq2seq prediction: each output feeds the next step. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.3)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the training section to the prediction/inference section, to make the contrast between teacher forcing during training and autoregressive generation during inference explicit.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the seq2seq prediction (inference) process: the encoder processes the source sequence, and the decoder generates tokens one at a time, feeding each predicted token as input to the next step, starting from a special beginning-of-sequence token and stopping at an end-of-sequence token.

image_metadata.caption: Context vector (size 4) bridges encoder and decoder in seq2seq. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem, right before explaining why attention was needed; helps students visualize what information is compressed into a single vector.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing the context as a fixed-size vector (size 4 shown) produced by the encoder RNN and passed to the decoder RNN, with hidden states illustrated at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN steps, updating hidden states sequentially. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how raw text is fed into an RNN encoder; use it to ground the abstract notion of 'hidden state' before moving to attention mechanisms.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings transforming discrete words into dense vectors, and showing how an RNN processes sequential inputs by combining an input embedding vector with the previous hidden state at each time step to produce a new hidden state.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heatmap shows learned French-English word alignments during decoding. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining how attention scores are computed, to demonstrate what the model has learned; particularly effective for illustrating that attention captures linguistic structure like word reordering.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap-style visualization showing attention weights between source (French) and target (English) words during decoding, illustrating which input words the model focuses on at each output decoding step, with non-monotonic alignments visible.

image_metadata.caption: Visual attention: high-res focus on key regions, low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?' to prime the concept.
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Each word 'attends' to others with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the visual attention analogy and before introducing the mathematical definition of attention, to transition from images to text-based attention.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weight visualizations showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence, with varying highlight intensities indicating attention strength between word pairs.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one context vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention — ask students 'what happens to early words when the sentence is very long?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — highlight that both query and keys come from the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity, showing which previous words the model attends to when processing the current word.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention — use the overlay to explain that soft attention is differentiable (weighted sum) while hard attention would select exactly one region.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating each word of the caption.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering Bahdanau attention and soft/hard distinction — use the diagrams to compare architectures side by side and discuss when local attention is preferable for long sequences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing Luong's global attention (left, attending over all source positions) and local attention (right, attending over a window of source positions centered on a predicted alignment point), both showing the decoder hidden state, attention layer, and context vector computation.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller reads/writes memory via soft attention. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts to memory-augmented networks — draw the parallel between NTM memory addressing and encoder-decoder attention weights explicitly.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) producing attention weight vectors over memory rows.

image_metadata.caption: Full NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After introducing each individual NTM addressing component (content, interpolation, shift, sharpening), show this diagram as a synthesis to help students see the full pipeline at once.
image_metadata.description: A detailed flow diagram showing the full addressing pipeline in a Neural Turing Machine, including content-based addressing, interpolation gating, location-based (convolutional) shift, and sharpening steps that produce the final attention weight vector w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads concatenated. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer architecture, introduce this diagram when explaining the core attention mechanism before discussing encoder/decoder structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) the scaled dot-product attention computation with Q, K, V matrices, softmax, and optional masking; (right) multiple parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention, residual connections, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer encoder specifically, after explaining multi-head attention, to show how attention is embedded within the full encoder block with normalization and residual connections.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder block showing the stack of: input embeddings with positional encoding, multi-head self-attention layer, add & normalize (residual connection), feed-forward network, and another add & normalize step, repeated N times.

image_metadata.caption: Complete Transformer architecture with encoder and decoder stacks. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After teaching encoder and decoder components separately, show this as a synthesis diagram to help students integrate their understanding of the full model.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right) side by side, with input/output embeddings, positional encodings, all attention layers, feed-forward layers, and the final output softmax.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL interleaves causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of pure attention models (weak positional encoding) and how hybrid architectures address them, particularly in the context of meta-learning or reinforcement learning.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information) used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention covers global spatial context.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or explaining the motivation for adding self-attention to convolutional architectures, to make the receptive field limitation of convolutions concrete and intuitive.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (limited local region) versus self-attention (global, all-to-all connections across the entire image), illustrating the difference in spatial coverage.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features projected to Q, K, V via 1x1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms generalize beyond NLP to computer vision, specifically when explaining SAGAN or non-local neural networks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing how image feature maps x are projected via 1x1 convolutions into query (f), key (g), and value (h) branches, combined via softmax to produce an attention map, then used to compute the self-attention output added back to the input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing globally coherent structure. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — illustrating why capturing non-local relationships matters for realistic image generation.
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the Transformer as a black box, to transition students from the external view to the internal component structure.
image_metadata.description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer as a black box: translates input sentence to output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer lesson to ground students in the practical application and establish the high-level input/output framing.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with an input sentence in one language entering and a translated output sentence exiting, labeled with encoder and decoder components.

image_metadata.caption: Six stacked encoders feed into six stacked decoders in the Transformer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why the Transformer uses multiple layers and how the encoder output connects to the decoder stack.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder-decoder architecture with six encoders stacked on the left and six decoders stacked on the right, with arrows showing information flow between the stacks.

image_metadata.caption: Single encoder block: self-attention layer feeds into feed-forward network. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward mechanics in detail.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows indicating data flow upward.

image_metadata.caption: Input words converted to 512-dimensional embedding vectors before encoding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared as input to the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through the encoder layers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing word embedding: input words (e.g., 'Thinking', 'Machines') being converted into fixed-size vectors (size 512) represented as colored bars, fed into the bottom encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Each word vector flows independently through self-attention then feed-forward layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing embeddings, when explaining how vectors travel through the encoder and why positions can be processed in parallel in the feed-forward layer.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how word embedding vectors flow through the two sub-layers (self-attention and feed-forward) of the encoder, with each word's vector following its own path through the encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Shorter sentence example tracing token paths through encoder sub-layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the high-level encoder overview to a detailed walkthrough of what happens inside each sub-layer, using a concrete short sentence.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a shorter example sentence being encoded, illustrating how each word's representation flows through the encoder sub-layers with arrows indicating the sequential and parallel processing paths.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', capturing long-range word dependencies. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the self-attention explanation to motivate WHY self-attention is useful before explaining HOW it is computed mathematically.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending to 'animal' with highlighted attention weights, demonstrating how self-attention links pronouns to their referents.

image_metadata.caption: Query, Key, Value vectors created by multiplying embeddings with learned weight matrices. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when beginning the mathematical walkthrough of self-attention, specifically to explain what Q, K, and V are and how they are derived from input embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, showing the dimensionality reduction from 512 to 64.

image_metadata.caption: Step 2: Raw attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing Q, K, V vectors, when explaining step 2 of self-attention — before normalization or softmax are introduced.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Steps 3–5: Scale by √d_k, apply softmax, weight value vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when walking through steps 3–5 of self-attention to explain normalization and the role of softmax.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the scoring process to show steps 3 and 4: dividing raw scores by √64 (scaling), then applying softmax to produce normalized attention weights that sum to 1, alongside step 5 of multiplying value vectors by these weights.

image_metadata.caption: Step 6: Sum weighted value vectors to produce self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the word-level self-attention walkthrough, just before transitioning to the matrix form of the calculation.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing step 6 of self-attention: summing the softmax-weighted value vectors to produce the final output vector z1 for the first word position, completing the self-attention calculation.

image_metadata.caption: Matrix form: X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V yields Q, K, V matrices. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after completing the word-level self-attention walkthrough, when introducing the matrix form to explain how transformers are implemented efficiently in practice.
image_metadata.description: Matrix calculation diagram showing how input embeddings packed into matrix X are multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query, Key, and Value matrices for all positions.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: eight parallel Q/K/V projections with distinct weight matrices. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention — use to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same Q, K, V calculation performed eight times in parallel with different weight matrices (W^Q_0…W^Q_7, etc.), producing eight separate Z matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Eight attention heads produce eight Z matrices requiring consolidation. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head Q/K/V diagram, to pose the 'how do we combine eight matrices?' problem before revealing the W^O solution.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the eight Z matrices (Z0–Z7) produced by eight attention heads, illustrating the challenge that the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix rather than eight separate ones.

image_metadata.caption: Concat eight Z matrices, multiply by W^O to produce single attention output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the resolution to the 'eight matrices problem', directly after presenting the Z0–Z7 outputs — completes the multi-head attention explanation.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how the eight Z matrices from multi-head attention are concatenated and then multiplied by a learned weight matrix W^O to produce a single output matrix passed to the feed-forward layer.

image_metadata.caption: Complete multi-head self-attention pipeline from embeddings to output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary after explaining all individual steps of multi-head attention — use as a reference diagram for review or when students need to see the big picture.
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing all components of multi-headed self-attention in one visual: input embeddings, eight sets of W^Q/W^K/W^V matrices, eight Z outputs, concatenation, W^O projection, and final output.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads reveal 'it' refers to 'animal' in coreference resolution. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-head attention mechanics, as a motivating real-world example — especially effective when discussing what attention heads learn or why multiple heads are useful.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing which tokens the word 'it' attends to across different attention heads when encoding the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads for 'it' — multi-head attention is hard to interpret. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual attention heads; use to show the student why multi-head attention is powerful but complex, and to prompt discussion about interpretability challenges in transformers.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to all other tokens, creating a visually complex web of attention weights across multiple heads.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (dim=4 example). (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; show this before the heatmap visualizations to give students an intuitive, low-dimensional example of how position vectors are combined with embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing input word embeddings (e.g., for 'You', 'are', 'amazing') each being added element-wise to a corresponding positional encoding vector of the same dimensionality (4 in this simplified example), producing position-aware input vectors.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings, color-coded. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding vectors; use as a stepping stone before showing the full 512-dimension heatmap to build intuition incrementally.
image_metadata.description: Small color-coded heatmap grid showing positional encoding values for a few word positions across a 4-dimensional embedding space, with values ranging from -1 to 1 represented by color intensity.

image_metadata.caption: Full 512-dim positional encoding heatmap; each row is one word position. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4-dim example; use to show the real-scale pattern and discuss how the frequency structure allows the model to generalize to longer sequences than seen during training.
image_metadata.description: Large color-coded heatmap with rows representing sequence positions (up to ~20 words) and 512 columns representing embedding dimensions, showing the sinusoidal positional encoding pattern with alternating bands of color that shift across dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Original paper's interleaved sinusoidal positional encoding pattern. (Vaswani et al. / Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the July 2020 update note; use to compare with the previous heatmap and explain that the paper interleaves sine/cosine signals rather than concatenating them.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper showing positional encodings with an interleaved sinusoidal pattern (alternating sine/cosine per dimension pair), visually distinct from the Tensor2Tensor concatenated version shown earlier.

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around each encoder sub-layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the residuals section; use to show the structural pattern before presenting the stacked encoder/decoder diagram, so students understand the building block first.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the self-attention sub-layer with a residual (skip) connection that adds the original input back to the sub-layer output, followed by a Layer Normalization step, then the feed-forward sub-layer with its own residual connection and Layer Norm.

image_metadata.caption: Two-layer Transformer with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual encoder residuals; use to zoom out and show the full 2-layer Transformer, bridging the sub-layer detail to the complete model architecture.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Transformer with 2 stacked encoders and 2 stacked decoders, each encoder and decoder block showing residual connections and layer normalization around self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, with arrows indicating data flow from encoder stack to decoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: Vector-level view of residual addition and layer norm after self-attention. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When a student needs a more granular view of the Add & Norm operation; use alongside the architectural diagram to connect the high-level pattern to actual vector arithmetic.
image_metadata.description: Detailed vector-level diagram of a single encoder's self-attention sub-layer, showing input vectors flowing into the self-attention block, the residual addition of the original input vectors to the attention output, and the resulting vectors entering layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Linear layer produces logits; Softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer produces its final word prediction — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to human-readable output tokens.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.

image_metadata.caption: One-hot encoding represents 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, especially before discussing loss functions that compare model output distributions to target one-hot vectors.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 at the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.

image_metadata.caption: Six-word vocabulary with one-hot target vector for training. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how training targets are formatted — particularly when setting up the loss function discussion and the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active for a specific word, used to illustrate target labels in training.

image_metadata.caption: Model output vs. target distribution — the basis of cross-entropy loss. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why we need a metric to measure the difference between predicted and target distributions.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the one-hot target label for the word 'thanks', illustrating how loss is computed by comparing the two distributions.

image_metadata.caption: Target probability distributions at each decoding step during training. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step training targets — after the single-word loss example, to scale up to realistic sentence-level training scenarios.
image_metadata.description: A sequence of probability distributions the model should ideally output at each decoding step for translating 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', showing one distribution per output token with the correct word having near-zero probability in an untrained model.

image_metadata.caption: Trained model outputs near-perfect probability distributions per decoding step. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the untrained/target distributions to contrast before-and-after training, and to introduce decoding strategies like greedy decoding and beam search.
image_metadata.description: Probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model at each decoding step for 'i am a student', showing high probability mass concentrated on the correct output word at each position, contrasting with the untrained model's distributions.

image_metadata.caption: Query–key scoring and softmax weights pool values into an output (D2L).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing attention for the first time or when a learner confuses keys vs. values; use it to walk through the three steps: score, normalize, pool.
image_metadata.description: A schematic of the query–key–value attention mechanism: a query q is compared against multiple keys k_i to produce attention weights (via a compatibility function and softmax), which are then used to compute a weighted sum of the corresponding values v_i (attention pooling) to produce an output.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla RNN seq2seq uses a single context vector bottleneck (Dive into Deep Learning).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after introducing vanilla seq2seq for translation, before explaining why attention is needed.
image_metadata.description: Sequence-to-sequence RNN encoder–decoder diagram where an encoder reads source tokens x1…xT into hidden states and compresses them into a single fixed-length context/state vector, which is then provided to the decoder to generate target tokens y1…yT'.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau attention computes a per-step context from encoder states (Dive into Deep Learning).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from fixed context to attention; use it to walk through one decoding time step end-to-end.
image_metadata.description: Detailed seq2seq-with-attention schematic: encoder produces hidden states at all time steps; at each decoder step, the previous decoder hidden state acts as a query over encoder states (keys/values) via additive (Bahdanau) attention to produce a context vector, which is combined with the decoder input to update the decoder state and generate the next token.

image_metadata.caption: Weighted skip connections form a context vector from encoder states (source: pantelis.github.io).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after explaining the limitation of using only the final encoder state, before introducing formal attention scoring/softmax; use it to motivate time-varying weights per decoder step.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of RNN encoder–decoder with attention-like skip connections: multiple encoder hidden states feed into each decoder step via weighted connections, producing a context vector as a weighted sum rather than relying only on the final encoder state.

image_metadata.caption: Attention at decoder step 1 emphasizes the source pronoun (source: pantelis.github.io).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the later-step figure to compare distributions; prompt the student to explain why the pronoun is most relevant at the first decoding step.
image_metadata.description: Step-specific seq2seq attention visualization for the same French→English example at an early decoder time (described as time step 1): attention weights concentrate on the source pronoun, forming the context vector used for the first target prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Machine translation as maximizing p(y|x) over variable-length sequences (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use at the start of a seq2seq lesson to define x and y, and when introducing the formal objective y* = argmax_y p(y|x).
image_metadata.description: A schematic of human vs machine translation: a source sentence x1..xm in one language mapped to a target sentence y1..yn in another, emphasizing variable lengths and the goal of maximizing conditional probability p(y|x).

image_metadata.caption: Encoder-decoder: encode source, then decode target autoregressively (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right before comparing model families (RNN seq2seq vs attention/Transformer) to anchor all variants in the same encoder-decoder template.
image_metadata.description: Encoder-decoder block diagram: an encoder consumes the source tokens and produces a representation; a decoder generates target tokens conditioned on that representation, illustrating the standard seq2seq framework.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq as conditional language modeling: predict y_t given y_<t and x (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when students already know LMs and you want to explain seq2seq training as the same next-token prediction objective plus source conditioning.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of a standard language model versus a conditional language model: LM predicts next token from previous target tokens only, while CLM additionally conditions predictions on a source input x.

image_metadata.caption: Decoder hidden state → linear layer → softmax gives p(y_t|y_<t,x) (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deriving the training loss or explaining how a decoder turns hidden states into token probabilities over the vocabulary.
image_metadata.description: A detailed CLM pipeline diagram: encoder processes source x; decoder produces hidden states for each timestep; a linear layer plus softmax outputs a vocabulary distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Basic RNN seq2seq: encoder final state initializes decoder (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use before introducing attention to highlight the limitation of compressing the whole source into one vector/state.
image_metadata.description: The simplest seq2seq model with two RNNs (often LSTMs): an encoder RNN reads the source sequence; its final state initializes the decoder RNN, which then generates the target sequence token by token.

image_metadata.caption: Last encoder states cluster sentences with similar meaning (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the encoder state to build intuition about what it encodes, or when discussing why seq2seq can generalize across paraphrases.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of sentence representations from the last encoder state: multiple example sentences plotted so that semantically similar sentences cluster together despite different word order/structure.

image_metadata.caption: Per-step cross-entropy: push probability mass onto the correct token y_t (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing the seq2seq objective or when students confuse sequence-level loss with per-token loss; walk through one timestep then sum over t.
image_metadata.description: Single-timestep training illustration: the model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary; the gold target is a one-hot vector for y_t; cross-entropy encourages high probability on the correct token.

image_metadata.caption: Inference is argmax over sequences; exact search is intractable (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right before introducing greedy decoding and beam search to justify why we need heuristics instead of exact maximization.
image_metadata.description: An equation-style graphic showing inference as an argmax over sequences: y' = argmax_y p(y|x, θ), highlighting that exact search over |V|^n sequences is infeasible.

image_metadata.caption: Greedy decoding can miss the best sequence; local argmax isn’t global (Voita NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when students ask why not just take argmax at each timestep; step through the example and compare total sequence scores.
image_metadata.description: A small example demonstrating greedy decoding failure: choosing the most probable token at each step leads to a worse overall sequence than an alternative with lower early probability but higher total probability.

image_metadata.caption: Fixed-vector bottleneck in vanilla seq2seq (source: lena-voita.github.io).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after introducing vanilla seq2seq to explain why translation quality degrades on long sentences.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of the seq2seq bottleneck: an encoder must compress an entire source sentence into a single fixed-size vector passed to the decoder, highlighting information loss for long/complex inputs.

image_metadata.caption: Attention step: scores → softmax weights → context vector (source: lena-voita.github.io).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when walking through one decoder step mathematically or in pseudocode, to align symbols with blocks.
image_metadata.description: Detailed attention computation scheme: encoder produces a sequence of hidden states; decoder state queries them to compute attention scores, applies softmax to get weights, forms a weighted sum context vector, and uses it to produce the next output.

image_metadata.caption: Popular attention score functions: dot, general, additive (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when choosing an attention variant for an implementation or when contrasting additive vs dot-product behavior and efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Comparison table/strip of common attention score functions used in early seq2seq models, typically including dot product, general (bilinear with a matrix), and additive/MLP (Bahdanau) forms, showing their formulas and required parameters.

image_metadata.caption: Luong (multiplicative) attention variant for NMT (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing Bahdanau vs Luong or when explaining why dot-product-style scoring is computationally attractive.
image_metadata.description: Luong attention model diagram: encoder hidden states are scored against the current decoder state using dot/general/concat scoring; attention weights produce a context vector; context is combined with decoder state (often via a linear layer + tanh) to form an attentional hidden state used for prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights form soft source–target alignments (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students learn how weights are computed, to practice reading attention maps and diagnosing whether a model attends sensibly.
image_metadata.description: Example attention heatmaps/matrices from NMT showing source words on one axis and generated target words on the other; darker cells indicate higher attention weights, illustrating near-diagonal soft alignments with occasional reordering.

image_metadata.caption: Modeling tradeoffs: RNN/CNN vs self-attention (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when motivating Transformers after seq2seq attention, especially to answer 'why not just use RNNs?' with concrete tradeoffs.
image_metadata.description: Comparison table contrasting RNNs, CNNs, and self-attention/Transformer along modeling dimensions such as maximum path length between tokens, parallelizability, and computational complexity per layer, emphasizing why Transformers train faster and capture long-range dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Original Transformer encoder–decoder overview (Google AI blog).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as an overview right before detailing self-attention and masking, or when students need to locate where cross-attention happens in the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Animated schematic of the original Transformer encoder–decoder: stacked encoder layers with self-attention and feed-forward blocks, stacked decoder layers with masked self-attention, encoder–decoder attention, and output generation; shows parallel token processing and attention connections.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer in words: parallel encoder, autoregressive decoder (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Transformer animation to confirm understanding, or when introducing why self-attention helps disambiguate word meaning using full-sentence context.
image_metadata.description: Word-based intuitive diagram of Transformer encoder/decoder behavior: tokens in the encoder interact simultaneously via self-attention; decoder generates tokens step-by-step while attending to encoder outputs, emphasizing 'all-at-once' contextualization in the encoder.

image_metadata.caption: RNN vs Transformer: resolving “bank” using global context (Voita NLP course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the practical advantage of self-attention for meaning disambiguation and why Transformers handle long sentences better than RNNs.
image_metadata.description: Illustration comparing RNN encoding vs Transformer self-attention on a sentence containing the ambiguous word 'bank' (river bank): RNN must process sequentially to accumulate context, while Transformer allows immediate token-to-token interactions to resolve meaning earlier via self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: Decoder self-attention vs cross-attention in a Transformer decoder layer (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When a learner confuses self-attention with cross-attention, or when introducing the decoder layer sequence: self-attend to prefix, then attend to encoder outputs.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side schematic contrasting decoder self-attention (tokens in the target prefix attending to each other) with encoder-decoder (cross) attention (decoder tokens attending to encoder hidden states). Shows information flow arrows from previous target tokens and from source representations into the current decoder layer.

image_metadata.caption: Q, K, V roles in self-attention: match queries to keys, mix values (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Right before presenting the attention equations, or when students ask why attention needs three projections instead of one.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of query, key, and value vectors derived from each token embedding/hidden state, with arrows indicating how a token’s query compares to other tokens’ keys to produce attention weights, which then weight the corresponding values to form the output representation.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled dot-product attention: softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from intuition to implementation, or when deriving tensor shapes for batched attention.
image_metadata.description: Compact formula diagram for scaled dot-product attention showing Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k) V, with labels indicating where scores, normalization, and value mixing occur.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: split Q/K/V into heads, attend independently, then combine (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: After students understand single-head attention and ask what ‘heads’ are or why they help capture different dependencies.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head attention setup where Q, K, and V are linearly projected into multiple heads (separate subspaces), attention is computed independently per head, and head outputs are concatenated and projected back.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer FFN: Linear → ReLU → Linear, applied per token position (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining what happens after attention in each layer, or when comparing attention (mix tokens) vs FFN (process per token).
image_metadata.description: Block diagram of the Transformer position-wise feed-forward network: a linear layer expanding dimensionality, ReLU activation, then a second linear layer projecting back to model dimension.

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection: add sublayer input to its output before the next step (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students ask what the ‘Add’ in ‘Add & Norm’ means, or when discussing training stability in deep Transformers.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of a residual (skip) connection around a sublayer, showing the sublayer input added to its output before passing onward (often into normalization).

image_metadata.caption: Layer normalization in Transformer: normalize each token’s hidden features (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When covering ‘Norm’ in Add & Norm, or when learners confuse normalization types and axes (batch vs layer).
image_metadata.description: Diagram explaining layer normalization within the Transformer’s Add & Norm block, indicating normalization of each token’s feature vector (across hidden dimensions) to stabilize activations.

image_metadata.caption: Input representation = token embedding + positional encoding to inject order (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing why Transformers need positional information, or before presenting sinusoidal positional encoding formulas.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of positional encoding/positional embeddings being added to token embeddings to form the Transformer input, emphasizing that order information is injected by summation.

image_metadata.caption: BPE subword segmentation: represent rare words as sequences of frequent subwords (Voita).
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining preprocessing for NMT/Transformers, especially if learners ask how models handle rare or unseen words.
image_metadata.description: Example illustrating word vs subword tokenization (Byte Pair Encoding), showing how a word can be split into frequent subword units and how sequences differ under word-level and subword-level segmentation.

image_metadata.caption: BPE training builds merge rules and a subword vocabulary (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how BPE learns merge rules from a corpus, before discussing how the same rules are applied at inference time.
image_metadata.description: Animated walkthrough of BPE training: starting from character-level word segmentations, counting frequent adjacent symbol pairs within words, repeatedly merging the most frequent pair, updating the merge table and token vocabulary, and showing resulting subword tokens with continuation marker (e.g., @@).

image_metadata.caption: Applying learned BPE merges to segment text at inference (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after presenting the learned merge table, when students ask how to tokenize unseen words or how @@ is used in practice.
image_metadata.description: Animated example of applying a learned BPE merge table to segment new text at inference: starting from characters, applying merges in learned priority order, producing subword tokens, and marking non-final subwords with @@ (distinguishing mat vs mat@@).

image_metadata.caption: Standard probing: predict labels from frozen representations (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from model-specific interpretability (e.g., attention heads) to model-agnostic analysis of representations using probes.
image_metadata.description: Schematic of the standard probing setup: take frozen model representations as features, train a lightweight classifier (probe) to predict a linguistic property/label, and evaluate what information is encoded in the representations.

image_metadata.caption: POS probing across encoder layers in NMT (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining what probes reveal about which layers encode POS/morphology, and when discussing why intermediate layers can be most informative.
image_metadata.description: Plot(s) of probing results for part-of-speech tagging: probe accuracy (or similar metric) versus encoder layer (embeddings, layer 1, layer 2), comparing how POS information changes across layers in an NMT encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Target language changes what the encoder encodes (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how changing the target language alters encoder linguistic knowledge, especially in lessons on morphology and inductive biases in NMT.
image_metadata.description: Comparison chart showing the effect of target language on source-side encoder representations: POS probing performance across layers for the same source language (e.g., Arabic) trained with different target languages, illustrating that target morphology influences what the encoder learns.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla LSTM seq2seq bottleneck on long inputs (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing limitations of early seq2seq models and before presenting attention as a solution.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating a basic LSTM seq2seq model without attention on long sequences: encoder compresses the whole source into a single final state/context vector, decoder generates the target from this bottleneck, highlighting difficulty with long-range dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Reversing source tokens shortens dependencies for decoding (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining historical tricks used before attention, or when students ask why reversing inputs improved early seq2seq results.
image_metadata.description: Before/after illustration of reversing source token order in seq2seq: original source-to-target alignment versus reversed source sequence, emphasizing shorter temporal paths between early target tokens and the most relevant source tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Subword segmentation example with BPE-style @@ markers (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing subword tokenization benefits (rare words, morphology) and before discussing stochastic segmentation methods like BPE-dropout.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of subword segmentation with BPE (and/or alternative segmentations) for the same word, showing how words can be split into subword units and marked with @@ to indicate continuation.

image_metadata.caption: Stochastic segmentation yields more morphologically consistent neighbors (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing BPE-dropout to justify why it helps, especially during discussions of embedding geometry and morphological generalization.
image_metadata.description: Nearest-neighbor comparison in embedding space: contrasts embeddings learned with deterministic BPE versus stochastic segmentation (e.g., BPE-dropout), showing that stochastic segmentation yields neighbors sharing meaningful subword parts more often than standard BPE.

image_metadata.caption: BPE-dropout: randomly drop merges to sample segmentations (Voita, NLP Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to implement BPE-dropout or when contrasting deterministic vs stochastic tokenization during model training.
image_metadata.description: Algorithm diagram for BPE-dropout: start from a learned BPE merge table, randomly drop a fraction of merge rules, then apply the remaining merges so that different valid segmentations are produced across passes.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder–decoder overview with attention blocks (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use at the start to give a roadmap before deriving Q/K/V and attention math; revisit when introducing cross-attention.
image_metadata.description: High-level Transformer architecture diagram showing an encoder stack on the left and a decoder stack on the right, connected via attention; includes token embeddings/positional encodings at inputs and output generation at the decoder side.

image_metadata.caption: Why attention helps: words depend on distant context (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before any equations to motivate why token-to-token interactions matter, especially for translation or coreference examples.
image_metadata.description: Sentence-level illustration motivating attention: a sequence of words where understanding/translation depends on relating non-adjacent tokens rather than processing strictly left-to-right.

image_metadata.caption: Q, K, V are linear projections of embeddings with defined shapes (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after introducing embeddings and before computing dot products; use it when students get stuck on dimension mismatches in code.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the three projection matrices used in attention: Wq, Wk, and Wv multiplying the input embedding matrix X to produce query (Q), key (K), and value (V) matrices, with shapes annotated (e.g., Wq,Wk ∈ R^{d_k×d}, Wv ∈ R^{d_v×d}).

image_metadata.caption: Single-position query example for self-attention (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when transitioning from defining Q/K/V to computing attention for one position; helpful before generalizing to full-matrix attention.
image_metadata.description: Illustration focusing on a single token position acting as the query (the second input element), showing how its embedding is projected to q(2) and compared against keys from all positions.

image_metadata.caption: Unnormalized attention weights from query–key dot products (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ω = QK^T (or its per-row equivalent) and before applying scaling/softmax; use to explain why each query attends to all keys.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-style visualization of unnormalized attention weights ω_{i,j} computed as dot products between a query vector q(i) and each key vector k(j), producing a row of scores for one query across all tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Scale by 1/√d_k then softmax to get attention weights (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when students ask why the sqrt(d_k) term exists or when demonstrating how raw scores become probabilities; pair with a small numeric example.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing scaling of ω by 1/sqrt(d_k) followed by softmax to produce normalized attention scores α, typically illustrated as a probability distribution over tokens for each query.

image_metadata.caption: Context vector as attention-weighted sum of values (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after softmax when explaining how attention produces new representations; use to bridge into the matrix form Z = AV.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of computing the context vector z(i) as a weighted sum of value vectors v(j) using the normalized attention weights α_{i,j}, yielding one output vector per query position.

image_metadata.caption: Single-head attention tensor shapes summary (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a recap after the full derivation or during coding exercises when learners encounter shape errors; keep visible as a reference sheet.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram listing the tensor dimensions for a single attention head, including X, Wq/Wk/Wv, Q/K/V, ω (or scores), α (softmax weights), and the resulting context/output vectors, with example sizes (e.g., d_q=d_k=24, d_v=28).

image_metadata.caption: Cross-attention in Stable Diffusion: text prompts condition image features (Raschka).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks 'where is cross-attention used in practice?' or when transitioning from encoder–decoder NLP examples to multimodal models like text-to-image generation.
image_metadata.description: An application-oriented diagram linking cross-attention to Stable Diffusion: text prompt embeddings condition image generation by attending between text features and image (U-Net/latent) features, illustrating cross-attention between two modalities (text and image latents).

image_metadata.caption: The Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on the Transformer to orient students to the overall architecture before explaining individual components in detail.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer model architecture diagram showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, and positional encoding, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, encoder-decoder attention, and feed-forward layers, connected by residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled Dot-Product Attention computation flow (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the attention mechanism formula; use alongside the equation Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V to connect math to computation graph.
image_metadata.description: Scaled Dot-Product Attention diagram showing the computation flow: inputs Q, K, V → MatMul(Q,K) → Scale by 1/sqrt(d_k) → optional Mask → Softmax → MatMul with V → output.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-Head Attention with h parallel attention layers (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining single-head scaled dot-product attention to illustrate how multi-head attention extends it by running h attention functions simultaneously.
image_metadata.description: Multi-Head Attention diagram showing h parallel scaled dot-product attention heads receiving linearly projected Q, K, V inputs, whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected to produce the final output.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention capturing long-distance verb dependencies (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing what self-attention learns in practice, especially to motivate why attention is preferable to RNNs for capturing long-range dependencies.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing colored lines connecting the word 'making' in an input sentence to other words across the sequence, with different colors representing different attention heads in encoder self-attention layer 5, highlighting long-distance dependency capture for the phrase 'making…more difficult'.

image_metadata.caption: Full attention pattern for head 5 showing anaphora resolution (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching about emergent linguistic structure in attention heads, particularly to illustrate how different heads learn different syntactic/semantic tasks.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization (top panel) showing full attention patterns for head 5 in layer 5, with lines connecting all words in a sentence, illustrating broad attention distribution relevant to anaphora resolution.

image_metadata.caption: Sharp isolated attention from 'its' resolving anaphora (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the full-attention panel (Image 5) to contrast broad vs. sharp attention patterns and reinforce the concept of head specialization.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization (bottom panel) showing isolated attention from the word 'its' for attention heads 5 and 6 in layer 5, with very sharp (concentrated) attention lines pointing to the antecedent noun, illustrating precise anaphora resolution.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention head learning sentence structure (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing what Transformers learn implicitly, especially in lessons comparing Transformers to syntax-aware models or discussing interpretability.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization from one encoder self-attention head at layer 5 showing attention patterns across a full sentence, with colored lines suggesting the head has learned sentence-structure-related behavior such as syntactic dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Second encoder self-attention head with distinct learned behavior (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show paired with Image 7 to highlight the contrast between two heads and reinforce the concept that multi-head attention captures diverse linguistic relationships simultaneously.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization from a second encoder self-attention head at layer 5 showing a different attention pattern across the same or similar sentence, contrasting with the first head to illustrate that different heads learn different tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Attention pooling: scoring function + softmax yields weighted value average. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on attention mechanisms, before introducing specific scoring functions like dot-product or additive attention, to establish the shared computational framework.
image_metadata.description: A flow diagram (Fig. 11.3.1) showing how attention pooling computes a weighted average of values. It illustrates the pipeline: queries and keys feed into an attention scoring function 'a', the scores pass through softmax to produce attention weights, which then weight the values to produce the output.

image_metadata.caption: DotProductAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor for minibatch size 2. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the DotProductAttention implementation and before students run the code themselves, to set expectations for what correct output looks like.
image_metadata.description: A code output visualization (likely a heatmap or tensor printout) showing the result of applying DotProductAttention to a toy minibatch with batch size 2, 10 keys/values of dimension 4, and valid lengths 2 and 6. Expected output shape is 2×1×4.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights from DotProductAttention with valid-length masking. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside image 2 when walking through the DotProductAttention example step by step, to illustrate intermediate attention weight distributions.
image_metadata.description: A second code output or visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or an intermediate result (e.g., softmax scores) for the same minibatch setup with valid lengths 2 and 6.

image_metadata.caption: Heatmap of masked attention weights: valid lengths 2 vs. 6. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how valid lengths prevent the model from attending to padded positions, using the contrast between the two batch examples (valid lengths 2 vs. 6) as a teaching moment.
image_metadata.description: A third output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of attention weights across the 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples, showing how valid lengths 2 and 6 concentrate attention on valid positions.

image_metadata.caption: Final DotProductAttention output: weighted value aggregation result. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the DotProductAttention example to tie together scoring, masking, softmax, and aggregation into one complete forward pass.
image_metadata.description: A fourth output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing the final output tensor values or a summary plot confirming the 2×1×4 output shape after weighted value aggregation.

image_metadata.caption: AdditiveAttention intermediate scores via tanh MLP. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal MLP computation of additive attention, to ground the abstract weight matrices W_q, W_k, w_v in a concrete numerical example.
image_metadata.description: A second output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or intermediate MLP activations for the same (2,1,20)/(2,10,2)/(2,10,4) setup with valid lengths (2,6).

image_metadata.caption: AdditiveAttention weight heatmap with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show side-by-side with the DotProductAttention heatmap to help students compare how the two scoring functions produce different raw scores but similar masked attention patterns.
image_metadata.description: A third output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of softmax attention weights showing how valid-length masking affects the distribution across 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples.

image_metadata.caption: AdditiveAttention final output: 2×1×4 context vectors produced. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final step of the AdditiveAttention walkthrough, then prompt students to compare this output with the DotProductAttention output to discuss similarities and differences.
image_metadata.description: A fourth output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing the final 2×1×4 output tensor after weighted value aggregation, completing the additive attention forward pass demonstration.

image_metadata.caption: Bidirectional RNN: forward and backward hidden states combined at each step. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing bidirectional RNNs, especially after presenting the fill-in-the-blank motivation example, to bridge the intuition of needing both past and future context with the actual architectural mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a bidirectional RNN showing two hidden layer sequences: a forward hidden state (H with right arrow) processing inputs left-to-right and a backward hidden state (H with left arrow) processing inputs right-to-left, with both concatenated to produce outputs at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Rolled RNN: module A recurrently passes hidden state h_t. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show first when introducing RNNs, before unrolling — ask students what the loop represents and what information it carries
image_metadata.description: A compact, rolled-up diagram of a recurrent neural network showing a single neural network module A with input x_t, output h_t, and a self-loop arrow indicating recurrent connection

image_metadata.caption: Unrolled RNN: identical modules share weights across time steps. (Olah, 2015)
