image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the rolled RNN diagram to demonstrate that the loop is equivalent to a chain; useful when explaining backpropagation through time
image_metadata.description: An unrolled recurrent neural network showing multiple copies of module A across time steps t-1, t, t+1, each receiving input x_t and producing output h_t, with arrows connecting hidden states sequentially

image_metadata.caption: Short-term dependencies: nearby context is easily captured by standard RNNs. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the long-term dependency problem; use as the 'easy case' before showing the long-term dependencies diagram to build contrast
image_metadata.description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating short-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context (shown in darker shading) is only a few steps away from the current prediction point

image_metadata.caption: Long-term dependencies: distant context is hard for standard RNNs to learn. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the short-term dependencies diagram; ask students why the distance between context and prediction matters before revealing the LSTM solution
image_metadata.description: An unrolled RNN diagram illustrating long-term dependencies, where the relevant prior context is many time steps away from the current prediction, with fading or distant connections indicating difficulty

image_metadata.caption: Standard RNN repeating module: a single tanh layer per time step. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show just before introducing the LSTM chain diagram; explicitly ask students to note how many layers are inside the module before switching to the LSTM version
image_metadata.description: An unrolled standard RNN repeating module showing a single tanh layer inside each time-step block, with input x_t, hidden state h_t-1, and output h_t

image_metadata.caption: LSTM chain: four interacting layers per module enable long-term memory. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the simple RNN module; use as a 'big picture' before walking through each gate — ask students to identify what is new compared to the simple RNN
image_metadata.description: An unrolled LSTM chain diagram showing repeating modules with four interacting neural network layers (yellow boxes), pointwise operations (pink circles), and both cell state and hidden state lines running across time steps

image_metadata.caption: LSTM notation guide: boxes, circles, and lines explained. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before any detailed LSTM gate diagram; return to it whenever a student seems confused by a symbol in a subsequent diagram
image_metadata.description: A notation legend diagram for LSTM diagrams showing: yellow boxes (neural network layers), pink circles (pointwise operations), single arrows (vector flow), merging lines (concatenation), and forking lines (copy operations)

image_metadata.caption: LSTM cell state: an information highway enabling long-range memory. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first explaining the cell state concept; use the conveyor belt analogy and ask students what advantage a direct path for information provides over the hidden state alone
image_metadata.description: An LSTM diagram highlighting the cell state as a horizontal line running across the top of the network through all time steps, with minimal interactions shown, emphasizing its role as an information highway

image_metadata.caption: LSTM gate: sigmoid layer controls how much information passes through. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing any specific gate (forget/input/output); ensure students can explain what a value of 0 vs 1 means before proceeding to the full LSTM walkthrough
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single LSTM gate showing a sigmoid neural network layer feeding into a pointwise multiplication operation, illustrating how gates control information flow with outputs between 0 and 1

image_metadata.caption: Forget gate: sigmoid decides what to erase from cell state C_{t-1}. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the first step in the LSTM walkthrough after explaining the gate notation; use the 'predicting next word after subject changes' example to ground the abstract mechanism
image_metadata.description: An LSTM diagram with the forget gate highlighted, showing a sigmoid layer taking h_t-1 and x_t as inputs and producing f_t, which is multiplied pointwise with the previous cell state C_t-1

image_metadata.caption: LSTM input gate: sigmoid selects updates, tanh creates candidates C̃_t. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the forget gate; use this to show the second of three LSTM gate operations before combining them in the cell state update.
image_metadata.description: LSTM diagram highlighting the input gate: a sigmoid layer (input gate) and a tanh layer producing candidate values C̃_t, showing how new information is prepared for storage in the cell state.

image_metadata.caption: LSTM cell state update: forget old info, add new candidate values. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After introducing both the forget gate and input gate; use this as the synthesis step showing how both gates jointly modify the cell state.
image_metadata.description: LSTM diagram highlighting the cell state update: the old cell state C_{t-1} is multiplied by the forget gate f_t, then added to i_t * C̃_t, producing the new cell state C_t.

image_metadata.caption: LSTM output gate: sigmoid filters cell state through tanh to produce h_t. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the cell state update step; use this to complete the full LSTM forward pass and explain what gets passed to the next time step and to downstream layers.
image_metadata.description: LSTM diagram highlighting the output gate: a sigmoid layer filters which parts of the cell state to output, the cell state is passed through tanh, and the two are multiplied to produce h_t.

image_metadata.caption: Peephole LSTM: gate layers receive direct cell state input. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After fully explaining the standard LSTM; introduce when discussing architectural variants and why researchers modify the baseline model.
image_metadata.description: LSTM variant diagram showing peephole connections, where additional lines feed the cell state C_t directly into all three gate sigmoid layers, allowing gates to condition on the current memory value.

image_metadata.caption: Coupled-gate LSTM: forget and input decisions are tied together. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing LSTM variants; use alongside the peephole diagram to show a spectrum of modifications from additive (peepholes) to reductive (coupled gates).
image_metadata.description: LSTM variant diagram showing coupled forget and input gates, where a single decision controls both forgetting and inputting: the input gate is replaced by (1 - f_t), so the network only writes when it forgets.

image_metadata.caption: GRU: two gates and merged cell/hidden state simplify the LSTM. (Olah, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After covering standard LSTM and its variants; use as a capstone comparison to show how architectural choices affect model complexity and state representation.
image_metadata.description: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) architecture diagram showing a reset gate and update gate, with merged cell and hidden state, illustrating a simpler two-gate recurrent unit compared to the standard LSTM.

image_metadata.caption: Context vector (size 4) bridges encoder and decoder in seq2seq. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem, right before explaining why attention was needed; helps students visualize what information is compressed into a single vector.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing the context as a fixed-size vector (size 4 shown) produced by the encoder RNN and passed to the decoder RNN, with hidden states illustrated at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN steps, updating hidden states sequentially. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how words are fed into an RNN encoder; use before discussing hidden states or attention to ensure students understand the input representation pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings transforming discrete words into dense vectors, and showing how an RNN processes sequential inputs by combining an input embedding vector with the previous hidden state at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights reveal which input words inform each decoded output word. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention mechanism mathematically or architecturally; use to demonstrate real-world learned alignment behavior and to motivate why attention improves translation of long or reordered sentences.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of attention weights across input sentence words at each decoding step, showing which source words the model focuses on when generating each target word, likely as a heatmap or highlighted sentence alignment for a French-to-English translation example.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq RNN encoder–decoder for machine translation. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing seq2seq models, before any code, to anchor the overall data flow from source to target sequence.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a sequence-to-sequence model showing an RNN encoder processing input tokens (e.g., 'They', 'are', 'watching') into a fixed-shape context vector, which initializes an RNN decoder that generates output tokens (e.g., 'Ils', 'regardent') one at a time, with special BOS/EOS tokens marking sequence boundaries.

image_metadata.caption: Decoder detail: context vector concatenated at each time step. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the decoder implementation, specifically when discussing how encoded information is passed to and maintained throughout the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram of the seq2seq decoder showing how the encoder's final hidden state initializes the decoder, and how the context variable is concatenated with the decoder input at every time step before being fed into the RNN layers, with a fully connected output layer on top.

image_metadata.caption: Training loss curve for the RNN seq2seq model. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the training loop code to demonstrate that the model learns; use to prompt discussion about loss curves and convergence criteria.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve (likely loss vs. epoch or iteration) for the seq2seq model on a machine translation dataset, showing the model converging during training.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq training loss curve (alternate framework). (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside other framework training outputs when discussing framework-agnostic implementation of seq2seq, or when students ask about reproducibility across frameworks.
image_metadata.description: A training output visualization, likely a loss or metric curve from a different framework implementation (e.g., MXNet, JAX, or TensorFlow) of the same seq2seq training run, showing convergence behavior.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq training loss (third framework implementation). (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as part of a multi-framework comparison discussion, or when emphasizing the importance of framework-agnostic algorithm design.
image_metadata.description: Another training loss or metric curve from a third framework implementation of the seq2seq training experiment, showing the model's learning progress over training steps.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq training loss (fourth framework implementation). (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final entry in a side-by-side framework comparison, or when summarizing the training section to confirm consistent results.
image_metadata.description: A fourth training loss or metric curve from yet another framework implementation of the seq2seq training, completing the set of multi-framework training result visualizations.

image_metadata.caption: Autoregressive seq2seq prediction: each output feeds the next step. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.3)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the training section to the prediction/inference section, to contrast with the training diagram and explain why inference is more complex.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the seq2seq prediction (inference) process: the encoder processes the source sequence into a context vector, then the decoder generates output tokens autoregressively, feeding each predicted token as the next input until an EOS token is produced, without teacher forcing.

image_metadata.caption: Visual attention: high-res focus on key regions, low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?' to prime the concept.
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Each word 'attends' to others with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the visual attention analogy and before introducing the mathematical definition of attention, to transition from images to text-based attention.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weight visualizations showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence, with varying highlight intensities indicating attention strength between word pairs.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one context vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention — ask students 'what happens to early words when the sentence is very long?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — highlight that both query and keys come from the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity, showing which previous words the model attends to when processing the current word.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention — use the overlay to explain that soft attention is differentiable (weighted sum) while hard attention would select exactly one region.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating each word of the caption.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering Bahdanau attention and soft/hard distinction — use the diagrams to compare architectures side by side and discuss when local attention is preferable for long sequences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing Luong's global attention (left, attending over all source positions) and local attention (right, attending over a window of source positions centered on a predicted alignment point), both showing the decoder hidden state, attention layer, and context vector computation.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller reads/writes memory via soft attention. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts to memory-augmented networks — draw the parallel between NTM memory addressing and encoder-decoder attention weights explicitly.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) producing attention weight vectors over memory rows.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content lookup → interpolation → shift → sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after introducing each individual NTM addressing component, or as an overview at the start of the NTM addressing section to give students a roadmap.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the NTM addressing mechanism showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing → interpolation with previous weights → location-based shift convolution → sharpening, producing the final attention vector w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and linear projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer's core attention mechanism; use the left panel first to explain single-head attention, then the right panel to explain parallelism and concatenation.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers stacked N times. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining multi-head attention in isolation, show this to demonstrate how it fits into the full encoder stack with residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention and positional encoding. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as an overview at the start of the Transformer section to orient students, and again as a synthesis diagram after covering encoder and decoder separately.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL combines causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning tasks. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer's positional encoding for reinforcement learning or meta-learning, show this as an example of a hybrid architecture that compensates with explicit temporal convolutions.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and causal attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information) used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention accesses all spatial positions globally.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or comparing CNNs to attention-based models, show this early to build intuition for why convolutions struggle with global context before explaining the self-attention solution.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the limited local receptive field of a convolutional operation (small highlighted region) versus the global receptive field of self-attention (entire image accessible), illustrating the spatial coverage difference.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features split into key, query, value via 1×1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Transformer's attention mechanism, show this to demonstrate how the same KQV framework applies to image feature maps, reinforcing the generality of the attention concept across domains.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing convolutional feature maps branched into three paths (f, g, h corresponding to key, query, value), attention map computation via softmax, weighted sum to produce attended features, and residual addition to input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showcasing self-attention quality. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention mechanism and the attention map formula, to give students a tangible payoff — use it to ask: 'What visual properties (e.g., fur texture consistency, symmetry) might require long-range attention to generate correctly?'
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals, demonstrating the visual quality and diversity of outputs achievable with self-attention mechanisms in generative adversarial networks.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla seq2seq uses a single context vector for decoding (Dive into Deep Learning).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right before introducing attention, when students ask why long sentences degrade translation quality.
image_metadata.description: A classic RNN encoder–decoder (seq2seq) diagram: source tokens are encoded into a fixed-dimensional context/state, which is then passed to an RNN decoder that generates target tokens step by step.
